title: mu-ovl-B: Implement OperatorView rendering rules per SpanKind
description:
Sub-bead of mu-ovl. Behavior phase. Replaces the shallow OperatorView prefix-tag at `renderer.rs:224` with per-SpanKind rendering rules that make AgentView and OperatorView visibly different.

## Scope

IN: in `FauxProviderRenderer::render_content` (`renderer.rs:221+`), match on `SpanKind` and produce distinct OperatorView output for each variant. Suggested rules (autonomous session may refine — record final choices in commit body):

| SpanKind | AgentView | OperatorView |
|---|---|---|
| System / User / Assistant | full content | full content |
| ToolResult | full content (raw JSON or text) | one-line structured summary — e.g., `[tool-result: ok, 3 items]` or first N chars |
| ToolCall | full content (name + args JSON) | name + arg keys only — e.g., `call: bash(command)` |
| ToolSchema | full schema JSON | name only — e.g., `[tool: bash]` |
| SkillActivation | full activation event | `[skill: <name>]` badge |
| MemoryInjection | full memory content | `<memory id=X collapsed>...</memory>` collapsible tag |
| Compaction | full synthetic span content | `[compacted N spans: <summary>]` |
| FileLoad | full file content | `[file: <path>] <N lines>` |

Add a `SpanKindMetadata` or inline policy table in renderer.rs documenting the rules.

Tests: hand-build a rope containing at least one span of each new kind, render under both targets, assert different output structures. The 'same input, two outputs' contrast is the load-bearing demonstration of the projection abstraction.

OUT: TUI integration (consuming OperatorView output in mu-pex / mu-jb3). File a follow-on bead `mu-ovl-tui` blocked-by mu-ovl-B for that work.

## Acceptance

- Per-SpanKind rule landed for all new variants
- Test asserting AgentView != OperatorView output for at least 4 variants
- Test asserting AgentView output is unchanged for the 4 original variants
- `cargo test --workspace` green at commit
- Commit body documents the final per-variant rule choices (if they diverged from this table)
- Follow-on bead `mu-ovl-tui` filed for TUI consumption

## References

- Existing renderer impl: `crates/mu-core/src/context/renderer.rs:215-235` (where render_content lives)
- Spec lines 614-644 (agent-view vs operator-view projections)
- Experiment doc: `~/.claude-personal/experiments/goal-2026-05-13d-mu-ovl.md`
close_reason: Landed in xszxyzyy 8920297c — per-SpanKind OperatorView rules: name/badge for tool/skill, collapsible for memory, summary for tool-result/compaction, byte-count for file-load. AgentView verbatim. Acceptance tests: AgentView unchanged for original 4, AgentView != OperatorView for 6 enriched kinds (>= 4 required). cargo test --workspace green; mu-core 136 → 141. mu-ovl closes; mu-ovl-tui follow-on bead filed separately.

title: mu-coding: bash env scrub whitelist is dead code — ENV_WHITELIST.contains() || !is_secret_env_var() is more permissive than intended
description:
## Problem

`crates/mu-coding/src/tools/bash.rs:293` filters which envvars pass
through to subprocesses with:

```rust
if ENV_WHITELIST.contains(&k.as_str()) || !is_secret_env_var(&k) {
```

The OR with the negation makes the whitelist dead code. Truth table:

| whitelisted | is_secret | (W || !S) | kept |
|---|---|---|---|
| yes | yes | true   | yes (whitelist wins) |
| yes | no  | true   | yes (whitelist redundant — !S also true) |
| no  | yes | false  | no  (correctly rejected) |
| no  | no  | true   | yes (whitelist redundant) |

Effective behavior: **anything not classified as secret passes,
regardless of whitelist membership.** The whitelist's only practical
contribution is keeping things named like secrets when explicitly
listed — but the current whitelist contents are mundane shell vars
(PATH, HOME, USER, TERM, LANG, etc.) that already pass `!is_secret`,
so the whitelist contributes zero filtering today.

## Security implication (real but narrow)

Whitelist-AND-not-secret (the more conservative form) would only pass
explicitly-named envvars. Whitelist-OR-not-secret (current) passes
anything `is_secret_env_var` doesn't catch — and the heuristic at
`bash.rs:70` catches common patterns (`*_API_KEY`, `*_TOKEN`,
`*PASSWORD*`, `*SECRET*`, `*api_key*`) but custom-named secrets
(e.g., `MY_PRIVATE_DATA`, `INTERNAL_CRED`, `WEBHOOK_URL`) leak through.

## Proposal

Pick one of:

(a) **Strict (probably intended):** change to `&&`:
    ```rust
    if ENV_WHITELIST.contains(&k.as_str()) && !is_secret_env_var(&k) {
    ```
    Now only explicitly-whitelisted vars pass, AND even whitelisted
    ones are rejected if they later acquire a secret-shape name.

(b) **Honest naming (current effective behavior):** drop the whitelist,
    just use `!is_secret_env_var(&k)`. Rename / document so it's clear
    the policy is "exclude secret-named, pass everything else."

The bash.rs strict-mode security posture suggests (a) was intended.
The test at bash.rs:470+ covers `is_secret_env_var` heuristic but
doesn't assert anything about whitelist filtering, so the test suite
didn't catch the dead-code condition.

## Acceptance

- Whitelist semantics match the implementation (or are documented to
  match)
- A test added covering: a non-whitelisted, non-secret-named variable
  (e.g., `FOOBAR=baz`) — passes under current code, would NOT pass
  under (a)
- Decision documented in the source comment near the condition

## Cross-refs

- Provenance: gpt-5.5 code review of mu, daemon f3845422 session-4
  (2026-05-16), "Bash tool" specific observations section
- Source: crates/mu-coding/src/tools/bash.rs:293 (condition) and :64
  (whitelist contents) and :70 (is_secret_env_var heuristic)
- Tests: bash.rs:470-478 (cover is_secret_env_var; don't cover
  whitelist filtering)
close_reason: Merged in PR #50 (3de16f2) — strict bash env whitelist

title: mu-proxy-bridge Phase 1: claude-proxy mirrors API traffic to mu event log (read-only, zero detection risk)
description:
**Phase 1 of a multi-phase design** to make claude-proxy the bridge between Claude's transcript stream and mu's event-sourced substrate. This phase ships pure observability: every request and response that flows through `claude_proxy` (127.0.0.1:3180) gets mirrored to mu's event log. Outbound bytes are NOT modified — zero detection risk to Anthropic OAuth grants.

## Why

Today, Claude's conversation state has no first-class persistence substrate. Workarounds include `/clear`, `/compact`, `/handoff`, post-compact reread, and `agent memory context` injection — all ad-hoc workflows around the fact that the transcript is passed by value on every API call rather than living in a queryable, replayable store.

Mu has solved this engineering problem for its own sessions: `event_log.rs`, rope substrate (mu-nat), `ProviderRenderer`, `CacheStrategy`, and `CompactionPolicy` (mu-kgu). All projections over an event stream. **If Claude's transcript stream were just another producer into mu's event log, every workflow primitive mu already has would apply for free.**

This bead is the lowest-risk first step: capture the traffic. Subsequent phases (active rewriting, replay-from-rev, /handoff replacement) become feasible only after this baseline exists. Each subsequent phase is a separately-scoped bead with explicit risk assessment.

## Layered architecture (context for this phase)

| Layer | Role | Detection visibility | Phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Proxy mirror** (THIS BEAD) | Bidirectional capture, persist+dedup as events | None — outbound bytes unchanged | 1 |
| **Hooks** (SessionStart, PreCompact, UserPromptSubmit) | Lifecycle markers; fire even when proxy is bypassed | None — sanctioned API | 2 |
| **MCP tools** | Explicit Claude-driven persist/query/checkpoint | None — normal tool use to Anthropic | 2-3 |
| **Proxy active rewrite** | Modify outbound bodies based on event-log queries | Real, unquantified — DEFERRED until measured need | 4 (optional, may never ship) |

The return-path observation forces the proxy as primary, not fallback: Anthropic's `/compact` beta returns the compacted context in the response body. Hooks can't see API responses; MCP can't see API responses. **Only the proxy can intercept inbound compaction returns** and ship them to the event log. So even if active outbound rewriting (Phase 4) never ships, inbound mirror via proxy is load-bearing for compaction-aware persistence.

## Scope

### In

- **claude-proxy gains a mu-RPC client** (Rust→Rust, both already in workspace). Connects to the local mu daemon over its existing transport (unix socket once mu-weg detached-mode lands; TCP/JSON-RPC interim).
- **Two new code paths in `proxy_handler`**:
  - After reading inbound request body and before forwarding: fire-and-forget `proxy.observed_request` event to mu (full body, with `Authorization` header redacted).
  - After receiving response from upstream and before returning to client: fire-and-forget `proxy.observed_response` event to mu.
- **Body unchanged on the wire.** Proxy continues to do exactly what it does today (strip auth, re-inject backend auth, optional model-map rewrite). The mirror is purely additive.
- **Failure tolerance**: if mu RPC is unavailable, proxy still serves the request. Mirror is opportunistic, not load-bearing for the proxy's primary function.
- **mu-core gains two new event kinds**: `ProxyObservedRequest` and `ProxyObservedResponse`, each carrying body bytes, timestamp, backend name, status code (response only), and a session correlation ID.
- **Backpressure**: bounded channel between proxy hot path and mu RPC sender; on overflow, drop oldest with a metric increment (`proxy_mirror_dropped`).
- **Rope-substrate dedup**: messages array grows monotonically across turns. Use mu-nat's rope substrate to share common prefixes structurally — same machinery mu already uses for its own session events. No separate diff implementation needed.
- **Readable-transcript projection**: a new mu projection that consumes proxy-observed events and renders a human-readable transcript view. Composes with existing rope substrate. Enables `mu transcript --session <id> --range <event-rev>` for debugging/audit.

### Out

- Active body modification (deferred to Phase 4 or never)
- Hook integration (deferred to Phase 2 — separate bead)
- MCP tool surface for Claude-driven persist/query (deferred to Phase 2-3 — separate beads)
- Replay/fork (Phase 3 — separate bead)
- Anthropic /compact return-body parsing into structured event (Phase 2-3, depending on shape)
- Cross-machine event-log replication
- Long-term retention policy (use mu's existing event-log retention; revisit if proxy traffic dominates volume)

## Acceptance

- claude-proxy depends on a mu-RPC client; no other new workspace deps
- Every request through `claude_proxy` produces exactly one `proxy.observed_request` event in mu's event log; every response produces exactly one `proxy.observed_response` event
- Authorization headers are redacted in mirrored events (regression test: synthetic request with bearer token; assert event body contains `Authorization: <redacted>`)
- Proxy continues to handle requests when mu daemon is unreachable (regression test: kill mu daemon, send request through proxy, assert 200 from upstream and proxy logs `mirror dropped, mu unreachable`)
- Backpressure: bounded channel size configurable; overflow increments `proxy_mirror_dropped` metric (verifiable via mgmt API `/metrics`)
- New mu projection `transcript --session <id>` renders the captured request/response pairs as readable conversation
- Rope-substrate dedup verified: 10 sequential conversation turns produce ~O(turns) total bytes in event log, not O(turns²) — measure and assert
- `cargo test --workspace` green (mu repo)
- `cargo test` green (claude-proxy repo)
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean (both repos)
- `cargo fmt --check` clean (both repos)

## Risk register

| Risk | Severity | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Mirror introduces latency on hot path | Medium | Fire-and-forget via bounded channel; proxy returns response without waiting on mu RPC. Measure p99 latency before/after. |
| mu daemon down → proxy degrades | Low | Bounded channel + drop-on-overflow + metric. Proxy never blocks on mu. |
| Bodies contain secrets beyond Authorization (API keys in tool results, etc.) | Medium | Phase 1 redacts only Authorization. File follow-up bead for secret-scanning projection (mu-3d8 already exists for this — cross-link). |
| Anthropic detects proxy presence somehow | Low | Phase 1 doesn't change wire bytes vs current proxy. If current proxy is OK, Phase 1 mirror is OK. |
| Event-log volume grows unboundedly | Medium | Rope dedup amortizes growth; mu's existing event-log retention applies. Add metric for proxy-event volume. |

## References

- claude-proxy source: `~/src/public_github/claude-proxy/src/proxy.rs` (especially `proxy_handler` lines 43-200, `strip_auth_headers` line 63, model-map rewrite line 114)
- mu event_log: `crates/mu-core/src/event_log.rs`
- mu rope substrate (dedup machinery): `crates/mu-core/src/...` (mu-nat — landed PR #13)
- mu compaction policies (composes Phase 4): `crates/mu-core/src/...` (mu-kgu tree)
- Cross-link: `mu-mh4` (session persistence read-side) — proxy events compose with this
- Cross-link: `mu-3d8` (secret-aware prompt/content scanning) — natural Phase 1.5 follow-up
- Cross-link: `mu-weg` (detached-mode unix socket) — preferred mu RPC transport once landed
- Provenance: discussion 2026-05-15 between operator and claude-personal session, after empirical proxy code review confirmed strip+re-inject behavior. Operator framing: "false-detection avoidance, not rule evasion."

## Phase plan (this bead is Phase 1)

- **Phase 1 (THIS BEAD)**: Mirror only. No risk. Standalone value (replay/audit/debug).
- **Phase 2**: Hook-side persistence — SessionStart/PreCompact/UserPromptSubmit hooks write lifecycle events to mu via mu-client CLI. Composes with Phase 1 for full session reconstruction. Separate bead.
- **Phase 3**: Replay/fork RPC + MCP tool surface for Claude-driven checkpoint/resume. Separate bead.
- **Phase 4 (OPTIONAL, may never ship)**: Active outbound rewriting via proxy. Requires explicit risk measurement first. Separate bead with risk assessment as gating criterion.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## 2026-05-15 design refinement (operator insight: MCP+proxy unification)

The proxy IS the MCP server, with shared state between the HTTP-intercept role and the MCP-tool role. This dissolves the friction described in the original bead's Phase 2/3 framing.

### Unified outbound + return flow

1. Claude calls MCP tool: `mu.proxy.register_rewrite(spec)` → proxy stores spec keyed to session/connection
2. Claude responds normally; claude-code POSTs full context via proxy
3. Proxy outbound: looks up cached spec, applies transform, forwards rewritten body
4. Anthropic processes, responds
5. Proxy inbound: applies return-path transform per same cached spec (e.g., reshape into compaction-metadata response that claude-code recognizes as "history compacted; replace local with X")
6. claude-code REPLACES local history per response metadata; Claude sees the compacted form in context; future requests carry only post-compact history

### Why this is the design

- Eliminates "restart required" friction from the prior MCP-only sketch
- Eliminates autonomous proxy rewriting (every rewrite is Claude-explicit via MCP call — clearer audit, less infrastructure surprise)
- Same wire-detection risk profile as Layer B autonomous rewriting, but cleaner operational risk: every rewrite has an MCP-call audit trail
- **Return-path transforms are invisible to Anthropic** (asymmetry: they don't see what we do with their response). "Fake Anthropic-compaction-shaped response using mu's own heuristic" becomes zero-wire-risk even if it involves fabricating response metadata.

### What this newly enables (Phase 3+)

- `mu.proxy.inject_context(events)` — pull events from mu's event log into next outbound
- `mu.proxy.replay_to(rev)` — reset history to an event-log revision (undo turns)
- `mu.proxy.checkpoint_now(label)` — register save point
- `mu.proxy.elide(pattern)` — strip content (secret redaction)
- `mu.proxy.diff_response(rewrite_a, rewrite_b)` — A/B test rewrites for detection-risk profiling

### Phase reshaping

- **Phase 1 (THIS BEAD)**: mirror only — UNCHANGED
- **Phase 2** (separate bead): proxy gains MCP-server capability + lifecycle hooks for write-side persistence
- **Phase 3** (separate bead): MCP tool surface for register_rewrite / inject_context / replay_to / checkpoint
- **Phase 4** (separate bead, OPTIONAL): autonomous-by-policy rewrite (still gated on detection-risk measurement)

Provenance: discussion 2026-05-15 with operator post-bead-creation, after the proxy-as-bridge design was already locked. The MCP+proxy unification was operator's insight; substantially improves the architecture.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## 2026-05-15 market-research: Anthropic ships consumer-side memory on claude.ai

Operator encountered a new claude.ai onboarding prompt today:

> **"Claude has memory"** — "Now Claude can make relevant connections across your chats. Memory includes your entire chat history with Claude."
>
> [Don't use memory] [Use memory]

Direct relevance to mu-f5k's design space. Anthropic is shipping their own memory layer at the *application* level (claude.ai consumer surface). This neighbors mu-f5k's proxy-bridge persistence work in non-trivial ways.

### Open questions to resolve before next mu-f5k design pass

1. **Is it API-exposed?** Look for `enable_memory: true` (or similar) in the Anthropic API docs / SDK. If yes, the proxy bridge has a new substrate to compose with or compete with. If claude.ai-only, the API surface stays where mu-f5k lives unchanged.

2. **Scope: per-account or per-conversation?** Per-account cross-chat memory would overlap with our `agent.sqlite` memory store. Per-conversation only would be more like our event-log per-session scope.

3. **Does it work on `claude agents` routines?** If yes, a routine like `claude-code-changelog-watcher` (trig_01XkYJgQ4hLsa35Y9Um296wa, created today) could *use* the memory feature to track "last seen version" across runs instead of the embed-in-prompt baseline we just shipped. **Operator note: this would solve our state-storage question, but it's overkill for a single-version-string state — Anthropic's memory is presumably broader than a KV.** Worth confirming the shape before pivoting.

4. **What does Anthropic do with the memory?** Storage scope, retention, deletion semantics, opt-in/out granularity. Operator-relevant for trust posture.

### Alternative-use-case interest (for v2+ phases of mu-f5k)

If memory IS API-exposed, mu-f5k's Phase 4 (active outbound rewriting) becomes more complex: the proxy would need to decide whether to ALSO modify Anthropic's memory layer, or only the request body. Two persistence layers, two write paths.

### Operator stance (2026-05-15)

> "I'm going to close the window without selecting either box on the web, because I don't know enough about the behavior to make an informed decision to turn it on or not."

Mirrors the **caution-first** posture from the auth-key trap incidents (see [[anthropic_base_url_alone_is_fine]], 2026-05-13/14 burns). Default off until behavior is understood; investigate; then choose with full info.

### Action items

- [ ] Investigate API exposure: search Anthropic API docs / SDK changelogs for "memory" flag/parameter
- [ ] Skim "About memory" doc Anthropic links from the prompt — record scope/retention/deletion semantics
- [ ] If API-exposed, file a phase-2-or-later bead on how mu-f5k composes with it
- [ ] Re-prompt operator on the claude.ai consent question once questions #1-4 are answered

### Provenance

Discussion 2026-05-15 ~10:55 EDT with operator while setting up the changelog-watcher routine. Routine ID: `trig_01XkYJgQ4hLsa35Y9Um296wa`. Operator hit the memory prompt at https://claude.ai/new while researching the routine page.

title: mu-cap-aws: Capability gains AWS-namespace axis + intersect()
description:
Add an AWS-capability axis to the existing `Capability` struct in `crates/mu-core/src/capability.rs`, with intersect() preserving the narrowing-only invariant.

This is the 'mu is ready' gate for the AWS integration described in `~/src/personal/mu-aws-sandbox-infra/docs/mu-integration.md` and the capability-broker pattern (memory `03b44633` — capability_broker_pattern_thaddeus_systems_2026_05_13).

## Scope

IN:
- New `AwsCapability` type (struct with `name: String` and `session_policy: Option<serde_json::Value>`). `name` matches the catalog at `~/src/personal/mu-aws-sandbox-infra/capabilities/aws.json` (e.g., `aws.scout.readonly`). `session_policy` field type-prepares the biscuit-shaped narrowing axis without committing to its use yet.
- Add `aws: Option<AwsCapability>` (or `aws: HashSet<AwsCapability>` — worker's call; see Design questions) to the `Capability` struct.
- Extend `Capability::intersect()` to include the AWS axis with narrowing-only semantics.
- Spec amendment in `specs/architecture/capability-delegation.md` describing the new axis. Folds into the same commit per per-repo 'spec-amend commit' pattern.
- Tests: same-name same/none session-policy passes; different-name behavior matches the worker's chosen semantics; expiry/tools/autonomy axes unchanged by AWS axis addition.

OUT:
- The capability-broker / judge-orchestrator runtime — separate beads, blocked-by mu-037 (peer.hello + mailbox).
- An `aws-recon` skill that actually USES this capability — separate bead.
- Cross-daemon biscuit serialization (the spec calls out future biscuit-auth swap; that's still future).
- General-purpose Capability::Foo enum refactor — the existing struct-of-axes shape is already more idiomatic for this pattern.

## Design questions (worker decides; record choices in commit body)

1. **Single `aws: Option<AwsCapability>` vs `aws: HashSet<AwsCapability>`.** A worker may legitimately hold multiple AWS capabilities at once (scout + s3-write). HashSet supports that; Option does not. HashSet is more flexible but requires hashable AwsCapability. Lean: HashSet, but worker may justify Option if it lands first.
2. **Different-name intersect semantics.** If parent has scout.readonly and child requests sandbox.build, what does intersect produce? Options: empty set (HashSet model), intersect-on-name-match-only (subset semantics), or None (refuse incompatible). Pick semantics that preserve narrowing-only.
3. **session_policy axis.** Carried but unused for v1. intersect() between two Some session_policies: leave deferred (worker may halt at this; spec the deferral).

## Acceptance

- New AwsCapability struct (or chosen shape) defined; `Capability` struct gains `aws` field
- intersect() updated to include AWS axis; narrowing-only invariant holds
- Spec amended in same commit; cite chosen shape rationale at the spec line that introduces the new axis
- Tests: serde round-trip for AwsCapability; intersect cases (same name, multiple names if HashSet, missing axis on either side, expiry interaction unchanged)
- cargo test --workspace green at commit
- One commit closes this bead

## References

Memory: `03b44633` (capability-broker pattern), `8b46733d`/`dac304e2` (Edit-needs-Read + loop-guard lessons).
Doc: `~/src/personal/mu-aws-sandbox-infra/docs/mu-integration.md` (esp. 'Proposed shape: typed Capability enum' section — note the existing code is struct-of-axes, not enum-of-variants; worker should integrate accordingly).
Code: `crates/mu-core/src/capability.rs` (existing Capability struct + intersect at line 132+); `AutonomyCapability` is the model for adding a new axis cleanly.
Spec: `specs/architecture/capability-delegation.md` (current shape + invariants).
close_reason: Landed in commit 73741606. AwsCapability struct + aws: HashSet axis on Capability + symmetric Capability::intersect() + spec amendment in one commit per spec-amend pattern. 13 new tests; INV-1 narrowing-only assertion across all 5 axes (including AWS) passing. session_policy intersect deferred. cargo test --workspace green (360 tests).

title: mu-semantic-code-tool: agent tool for semantic code lookup (treesitter and/or LSP/rust-analyzer)
description:
Placeholder for a semantic code-understanding tool surface available to agents (alongside the existing read/grep/glob/edit/bash/write primitives). Motivation: spawns currently see code as tokens via grep; want them to see structure (functions, scopes, definitions, callers) when that matters.

## Two possible implementations

**Treesitter (syntactic):**
- Fast, language-agnostic, stable parser output
- Good for: "find all functions matching pattern X", "find all impl blocks for trait Y"
- Limits: can't resolve identifiers — two references to `foo` look the same even if they bind to different definitions
- Cost: pick a Rust binding (tree-sitter-rust crate), define a query DSL surface for agents, wire as new tool in crates/mu-coding/src/tools/

**LSP / rust-analyzer (semantic):**
- Slower to start, real semantic analysis (scope, types, definitions, references)
- Good for: "find the definition of this symbol", "find all usages of this function"
- Limits: Rust-only; one daemon per workspace; cold-start cost (~10s warm-up)
- Cost: integrate with rust-analyzer's LSP server, or a slim subset (textDocument/definition, textDocument/references)

The two are complementary, not exclusive. Different jobs:
- Treesitter: "find this pattern in code" (syntactic, language-agnostic)
- LSP: "find the definition of this symbol" (semantic, language-specific)

## Trigger conditions for actually building this (defer until at least one fires)

1. An autonomous /goal run silently misses something structural with grep and burns budget down the wrong path (concrete failure attributable to grep's limits, not the worker's regex error).
2. Multi-language work enters mu's scope (treesitter's superpower is cross-language uniformity; until then rust-analyzer covers Rust better).
3. A refactoring pattern recurs enough across beads to be worth a tool (e.g. repeated 'find all callers of this fn' surveys; today's mu-j97 was the first hint).

## Out of scope

- Building either implementation now. This bead is a placeholder + design notes for when one of the triggers fires.
- AST-aware Edit primitives. Possible follow-on once a base structural tool exists.
- Tree-sitter for non-Rust languages (mu-coding's prompt-engineering primitives ARE multi-language but the current call surface assumes file content).

## Acceptance (when this is actually picked up)

- Choose one of the two implementations (or both, as separate tools)
- Wire as a new `crate::tools::*` module in mu-coding with the existing Tool trait shape
- Define the query/output surface that agents see (string-in, structured-out)
- Tests + spec doc following the per-repo convention

## Notes

- Motivation discussed 2026-05-13: Thaddeus wants ways to help spawns 'look at things semantically rather than just a bunch of tokens.' Not urgent; filing while it's named.
- Compare cost/benefit: see also mu-pex (metrics framework) — both are agent-tooling-shaped beads; ordering depends on which agent-failure mode bites first.
- Related: existing mu-coding tools live in crates/mu-coding/src/tools/{bash,edit,grep,read,write,glob}.rs — the integration point is straightforward.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
**Scope expansion noted 2026-05-13 after surveying Crush** (https://github.com/charmbracelet/crush). Crush lists 'LSP-Enhanced: uses LSPs for additional context, just like you do' as a first-class feature. This is a STRONGER framing than the 'agent-queryable structural tool' shape I originally captured.

Two distinct primitives in play:

1. **LSP / treesitter as an agent-queryable TOOL** — agent asks 'find the definition of X', gets back file:line + snippet. Pull model. (Original bead scope.)

2. **LSP / treesitter as a CONTEXT-ENRICHMENT layer** — when the agent loop assembles context for a model call, automatically include relevant definitions, type signatures, or callers based on what the conversation references. Push model. (Crush's framing.)

The push model is more powerful but bigger: it requires the agent loop (or a context-assembly layer in mu-coding) to know what's worth enriching from the current conversation. That's a substantial architectural addition beyond a simple Tool trait impl.

When picked up, decide which (or both) primitives mu wants. The pull model is easier to add incrementally; the push model is what makes 'agents that see semantic code' really shine.

title: Adopt ProviderRenderer + CacheStrategy in live agent loop (mu-ktq follow-on)
description:
Final follow-on for the mu-ktq trait suite. Wire the new `ProviderRenderer` + `CacheStrategy` traits into the live agent loop so the rope-based context substrate replaces the current `&[AgentMessage]` flow.

## Blocked by

- mu-bn4 (AnthropicProviderRenderer + AnthropicCacheStrategy) — needs the Anthropic impls before the loop can switch over.
- Likely also mu-nat (full RetainedRope) — the stub used by mu-ktq is not load-bearing enough for the live loop. The full rope is needed so events accumulate into the retained pointer set rather than being assembled into a Vec<AgentMessage> ad-hoc.

## Scope

In:
- Replace the loop's per-turn `Vec<AgentMessage>` assembly with a `RetainedRope` projected through the appropriate `ProviderRenderer`.
- Plumb `CacheStrategy` selection (per-session config: NoCacheStrategy vs AnthropicCacheStrategy based on provider).
- Emit a `ContextAssembly` event (per spec lines 167-228) recording the rope state and the renderer/strategy used.
- Tests: end-to-end loop test that asserts the same model-visible payload comes out of the new path as the old.

Out:
- Operator-view consumers (`mu-ovl`) — separate bead.
- Subagent handoff via shared event log (`mu-x9j`) — separate bead.

## Acceptance

Live loop uses ProviderRenderer + CacheStrategy; cargo test --workspace green; integration test confirms no regression in produced provider payloads; spec-amend commit if the wire/RPC surface shifts at all.

## References

- mu-ktq (closed; foundational traits)
- mu-bn4 (Anthropic impls; blocks this)
- mu-nat (full RetainedRope; blocks this)
- specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md

title: mu-7rk-c: Capability enforcement gate — block non-auth RPCs without authentication
description:
**mu-7rk decomposition phase C — THE SECURITY PROPERTY.** Without this bead, the auth handshake is "security theater" (codex's term). This is the load-bearing bead in the decomposition; **P1**.

## Why

Codex's central blocker on the original mu-7rk:

> "AuthState is passed into dispatch but is only written on successful auth; no non-auth RPC checks it. Unauthenticated clients can call create_session, session.*, mailbox.*, daemon.* exactly as before. Explicitly denied clients can keep using the connection too."

The implementer's note — "v1 does NOT yet enforce 'must authenticate before session.* RPCs'; that gate is a clean follow-up bead" — was rejected by codex: *"This is the central security property. Without a gate, Capability::root is decorative and AuthState is unused."*

This bead is the gate.

## Scope

### In

- `crates/mu-coding/src/serve/dispatch.rs`:
  - Add an **auth-required check** at the top of every protected RPC handler. Before any handler logic, check that `AuthState::Authenticated { capability: _ }` is the connection's current state. If not, return JSON-RPC error with structured `auth_required` code.
  - Define an **explicit allowlist of pre-auth RPCs** in code: `peer.auth_offer`, `peer.auth_initiate`, `peer.auth_response`. **All other RPCs require authentication.** (The original `peer.hello` may stay public if explicitly desired — surface this decision in the bead's design notes during implementation.)
  - Add `AuthState::Denied { code: AuthDenialCode }` variant — terminal state. Once denied, the connection rejects ALL subsequent RPCs (including auth retries) with `auth_denied` until the connection is closed.
  - On `peer.auth_initiate` returning `Denied`, transition `AuthState` to `Denied{code}`. **Do NOT close the connection** — that's mu-7rk-d. This bead just makes the state terminal at the dispatch layer.
- `crates/mu-coding/tests/auth_smoke.rs` (modify; the v0 bypass-pinning test goes away):
  - **REMOVE** v0's `unauth_can_call_session_create` (or whatever name) — that codifies the bypass per codex blocker #3
  - **ADD** `unauthenticated_session_create_is_rejected` — proves the gate works
  - **ADD** `unauthenticated_session_list_is_rejected`
  - **ADD** `unauthenticated_mailbox_post_is_rejected`
  - **ADD** `unauthenticated_daemon_stats_is_rejected`
  - **ADD** `authenticated_session_create_succeeds` — proves the gate doesn't break the happy path
  - **ADD** `denied_then_session_create_is_rejected` — proves Denied is terminal
  - **ADD** `denied_then_auth_initiate_is_rejected` — proves Denied also blocks auth retries

### Out

- Connection close on Denied — mu-7rk-d (this bead just transitions state; transport close is its own concern)
- Per-RPC capability granularity (e.g., "this token can call session.* but not daemon.*") — mu-7rk-f's token-to-capability table
- Rate limiting — mu-7rk-f
- Client-side updates to handle `auth_required` errors — mu-7rk-e

## Acceptance

- All eight test cases above pass
- `cargo test --workspace` green; existing peer.hello + mailbox flows still work for AUTHENTICATED connections
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean
- `cargo fmt --check` clean
- No new workspace deps (gate uses existing types)
- The pre-auth allowlist is a single visible constant or match arm in dispatch.rs — easy to audit, easy to extend

## Universal don'ts

- Do NOT close the connection on Denied (that's mu-7rk-d)
- Do NOT silently allow any non-allowlisted RPC to bypass — fail closed by default
- Do NOT change the auth handshake mechanics from mu-7rk-b
- Do NOT add capability granularity beyond "authenticated vs not" (that's mu-7rk-f)

## References

- Codex review (the central finding): `~/.claude-personal/experiments/codex-review-mu-7rk-output.txt`
  - Blocker #1 (capability gate not enforced)
  - Blocker #2 (Denied not terminal)
  - Blocker #3 (test pinning the bypass)
  - Security_assessment: capability_gate_v1_severity = blocker
- Original bead: mu-7rk (closed)
- Reference v0 diff: jj rev `twztzykv 78978fdb` (the test that needs replacing is in `auth_smoke.rs:225` per codex)
- Depends on mu-7rk-b for `AuthState` shape and the handshake handlers it consumes
close_reason: Merged in PR #69 — the security property. Enforcement gate + AuthState::Denied + AUTH_REQUIRED/AUTH_DENIED codes + trust-on-spawn (MU_BEARER_TOKEN) for mu ask. Completes mu-7rk-c.

title: claude-code in pot/podman orchestration (Max5 OAuth, not API key) — design pickup
description:
## Tracking bead for the claude-in-pot integration

Session 2026-05-17 established:
- The 'integrate claude-code with agent-spawn etcd-leased pots' goal
- That the proxy-injection shortcut violates Anthropic ToS (OAuth must originate from claude-code itself, see memory a335a5dd)
- That naive nullfs of /compat/linux into pot fails Bun init
- That podman-as-host-user runs 'claude --version' but 'claude -p' exits silently (Bun runtime init under FreeBSD Linuxulator + possibly per-machine OAuth binding)

Full status in memory 8a455605 (claude_in_pot_integration_status_2026_05_17).

## Pickup options

A. **Warden-pattern install inside pot template**: copy /compat/linux into agent-runtime/m/, set devfs_ruleset 3001, snapshot, retry. Heavy template surgery; may still hit the same OAuth-context issue.
B. **Host-side claude-code with stdin/stdout shim**: pots/podman containers connect to a host-side claude-code via FIFO or socket. Loses container isolation but uses OAuth where it works.
C. **Park until upstream support**: accept that Max5 OAuth orchestration stays single-host. Use the existing agent-spawn (mu/pi) for everything else.

Operator preference: do NOT pursue the ANTHROPIC_API_KEY shortcut.

## Acceptance (whichever path)

- AGENT_SPAWN_RUNTIME=claude works end-to-end through agent-spawn-v2 with etcd slot leasing
- claude-code uses Max5 OAuth (not API key)
- 'agent-spawn-v2 "what is 2+2"' returns a real claude response

## Don't re-do

- Nullfs-from-host /compat/linux into pot template (proven to silently break Bun)
- mu-pointed-at-claude-proxy (Anthropic ToS violation per memory a335a5dd)
- claude --dangerously-skip-permissions under sudo (the flag refuses root)

## Cross-refs

memory 8a455605 (status), a335a5dd (policy), 9d1a3127 (proxy), 17063e32 (podman cleanup)

title: Cost-calc wiring: surface real $ in mu-tui F1/F3/F5 (depends mu-yz48 fix)
description:
## Goal

main.rs:1448 currently has 'cost_usd: 0.0, // populated when usage→cost mapping lands' and the budget tuple at line 376 is hardcoded (0.0, 10.0). Wire it up.

## Inputs available after mu-yz48 fix

Per-message Anthropic usage has the right fields: input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens. cumulative_usage in event_log already aggregates them across Done events.

## What needs landing

1. A ProviderPricing table — per-model rates. Initial set:
   - claude-opus-4-7: $5/MTok input, $25/MTok output, 1.25x cache-write, 0.10x cache-read (operator-confirmed 2026-05-17 from Anthropic pricing page)
   - claude-sonnet-4-6: $3/MTok input, $15/MTok output
   - claude-haiku-4-5: $1/MTok input, $5/MTok output
   - openai_codex: TBD
   - openrouter: TBD per-model
2. A cost(usage, pricing) helper that sums the four buckets.
3. mu-tui consumes it in build_session_row (replace cost_usd: 0.0) and in the daemon-stats poll (sum across sessions).
4. Budget tuple becomes config-driven (currently hardcoded $10).

## Calibration anchor

Operator's 2026-05-17 day-total of $13.04 reconciles to opus-4-7 $5/$25 pricing only after mu-yz48 fixes output_tokens. That's the regression target — bench against real days.

## Why P1

User-visible metric. With mu-yz48 fixed, mu-tui showing $0.00/$10.00 budget on a real session is now embarrassing rather than 'feature pending.'

## Composes with

- mu-yz48 (PR #75) — depends on the output-token fix landing for cost to be meaningful.
- mu-pex (timing + quality proxies) — pricing slots into the same metrics framework.

## Source

Operator observation + opus-4-7 review of F5 metrics, 2026-05-17.

title: mu: accountable work runtime — unified Actor/Principal model + multi-axis budget ledger + capability lifecycle events + workspace identity + kill switches [umbrella, north star]
description:
## Provenance

Architectural north star from gpt-5.5 conversation in mu daemon
f3845422 session-4 (2026-05-16), prompted by user's ATD trader/model
analogy at event 366. gpt's response at event 371 lays out the full
vision.

## Vision

Normalize humans, LLM sessions, delegated workers, tool workers,
scheduled jobs, and external services into **a single accountable
operational substrate**. The ATD trading-system pattern is the
architectural north star.

> "At ATD, the important architectural move was probably not 'traders
> used GUIs.' It was: a human trader was normalized into the same
> operational substrate as an automated model."

For mu: do the same for humans, agents, and tools.

## Trading-system mapping (verbatim from gpt event 371)

| Trading system | mu / agent system |
|---|---|
| Trader/model identity | Session/actor identity |
| Capital allocation | Token/tool/AWS/cost budget |
| PnL | Task outcome/value/cost accounting |
| Risk limits | Capabilities/policies/approval gates |
| Order gateway | Tool/runtime broker |
| Exchange connectivity | Providers/AWS/GitHub/filesystem |
| Position/risk book | Current workspace/context/resource state |
| Blotter | Event log |
| Compliance/audit | ContextAssembly/tool/CloudTrail joins |
| Human trader GUI | Human frontend/TUI/web UI |
| Automated strategy | Autonomous/delegated agent |
| Kill switch | cancel_session/cancel_outstanding/revoke capability |

The shared substrate is the key.

## Five concrete abstractions

### 1. Actor/principal model

```rust
ActorId
ActorKind::{Human, LlmSession, ToolWorker, ScheduledJob, External}
ActorLineage
ActorCapability
ActorBudget
```

Evolve sessions + parent/child lineage into an explicit actor model
covering humans and tools, not just LLM sessions.

### 2. Unified resource ledger

Project events into a ledger across multiple axes:
- tokens (input/output, cached)
- dollars (provider / AWS / human-attention proxies)
- wall-clock time
- tool calls
- AWS sessions / actions / mutations
- filesystem writes
- approval waits / human-attention cost

Partially present (usage stats per session); needs to be a first-class
projection that aggregates across actors and over time.

### 3. Capability lifecycle events

```text
CapabilityGranted
CapabilityAttenuated
CapabilityMaterialized
CapabilityRevoked
CapabilityDenied
```

Extends mu-j7r (approval-lifecycle events filed earlier this session)
into a fuller capability event family. Every grant, attenuation, and
revocation auditable.

### 4. Workspace/resource identity

Link each actor to concrete compartments:
- workspace_id
- jail_id
- zfs_snapshot
- aws_sts_session_name
- git_branch / worktree

The bridge between mu's logical identity model and the substrates'
enforcement (OS via the sandboxing bead; cloud via the AWS bead).

### 5. Limits and kill switches

Triggers: budget exhausted / risk threshold tripped / failure streak /
approval timeout / anomalous tool request / provider degraded / cost
spike.

Responses: pause / escalate / revoke / terminate / snapshot for review.

## Multi-axis budget vocabulary

```text
TokenBudget
CostBudgetUsd
WallClockBudget
ToolCallBudget
AwsMutationBudget
FilesystemWriteBudget
NetworkBudget
HumanAttentionBudget
RiskBudget
```

Examples:
- This scout: 50k tokens, read/grep/glob only
- This AWS worker: aws.scout.readonly only
- This builder: tagged sandbox resources up to ~$10/month
- This autonomous session: 5 iterations or 20 minutes
- This child: may request approval but not self-approve
- This worker: writes only inside a cloned worktree/jail

## Agent PnL concept

Rough form even without perfect quantification.

Costs: input/output tokens, provider $, wall time, tool calls, cloud
resources, human approvals requested, failed attempts, retries, review
burden.

Benefits: tests passing, patch accepted, issue closed, report
generated, recon completed, plan approved, resource cleaned up, docs
updated, incident diagnosed.

Example ledger entry:
```text
session X:
  goal: split protocol.rs
  cost: $1.42, 38k input, 7k output, 12 tool calls
  artifacts: patch, tests, summary
  result: accepted with edits
  human review: 18 min
```

Over time: identify which agent/task shapes are profitable.

## End-state framing

Not just "agent runtime" but **"accountable work runtime"** where
humans, agents, and tools all operate through visible capabilities,
budgets, ledgers, and workspaces.

The deep frame:
> "Normalize humans and automated actors into the same accountable
> operational substrate. Not because humans and agents are equivalent
> morally or cognitively — because the system tracks work through
> common primitives: who/what acted, under what authority, against
> what budget, in what context, producing what evidence, with what
> result."

## Sub-beads to spawn

Multi-year-scale work. Sub-beads as pieces crystallize:
- ActorId / ActorKind / ActorLineage typed model
- Unified resource ledger projection
- Capability lifecycle event family (extends mu-j7r)
- Workspace identity registry
- Kill-switch policy framework
- PnL ledger projection + reporting

## Cross-refs

- mu-j7r (approval-lifecycle events — narrower predecessor)
- mu-vkx (per-tool approval policy legibility — inside this vision)
- mu-hh1 (SupervisorCapability axis — substrate)
- mu-pex (metrics framework — overlaps with unified ledger work)
- OS-enforced sandboxing umbrella (sister substrate concern)
- AWS-capability integration umbrella (sister substrate concern)
- Memory: ATD trader-model analogy + accountable-work-runtime north
  star (filed same session — STRATEGIC ANCHOR; read this for design
  rationale)
- Provenance: gpt-5.5 architectural conversation, daemon f3845422
  session-4 event 371 (2026-05-16)
