title: mu-tui-markdown: render assistant responses as styled Markdown (termimad or equivalent)
description:
Render assistant Markdown responses in mu's TUI with proper styling: syntax-highlighted code blocks, tables, lists, emphasis, links. Today the TUI renders as plain text. Toggle between rendered and raw views for cut/paste workflow.

## Motivation

Assistant responses ARE Markdown — code blocks, lists, tables, headers, inline code, etc. Rendering them as plain text loses substantial information density. Concrete UX upgrade.

Operator note 2026-05-14: provider/model switching now working end-to-end with streaming updates in both F1 windows. Next-most-impactful TUI win is structured markdown rendering. Operator explicitly requested a raw/rendered toggle so cut/paste can grab the raw source (not the styled-but-unselectable-for-pipe rendering).

## Approach

Rust analogs to Charmbracelet's Glamour (Go):
- `termimad` (https://github.com/Canop/termimad) — most mature Markdown-in-terminal renderer in Rust. Used by broot. Direct dependency candidate.
- `pulldown-cmark` + custom renderer — finer control but more code; only worth it if termimad's defaults don't fit.

Termimad outputs styled text that can be embedded in ratatui via `ratatui::text::Text` / `Line` / `Span`. Compatibility: termimad uses crossterm under the hood, same as ratatui, so colors compose.

## Scope

IN:
- Add `termimad` (or chosen lib) to mu-tui's Cargo.toml
- Render assistant message body through termimad before display
- **Toggle between rendered and raw views** — operator-controlled, per-session. Suggested binding: `R` in transcript view (next to existing F-key family). Default = rendered. Toggle should not lose scroll position or re-flow disruptively.
- Test rendering with: code blocks (multiple languages), tables, nested lists, inline code, emphasis, links
- Theme alignment with mu-u8a's color polish work (moonfly/github_dark family)

OUT:
- Streaming Markdown rendering (rendering partial Markdown as deltas arrive — termimad expects complete blocks). v1: render on session.done event; show raw during streaming. Cleanly avoids the half-rendered-table flicker problem.
- Inline images / VHS-style embedded media

## Design notes

Streaming + raw/rendered interaction: the simplest mental model is "raw while streaming, rendered when done." That gives the operator a stable visual transition (yellow streaming block stays raw text → settles to white styled markdown when complete). The toggle then lets the operator force raw on a completed message for cut/paste. Two states × two phases = matrix to test:

| Phase | Toggle=rendered | Toggle=raw |
|---|---|---|
| Streaming | raw (still streaming) | raw |
| Done | rendered | raw |

This avoids needing partial-markdown rendering altogether and matches what an operator actually wants ergonomically.

Cut/paste compatibility: terminal selection grabs whatever cell content is in the cells. termimad renders styled-text into cells; the *displayed text* is the markdown source minus syntax markers (e.g. `**bold**` → `bold`). For accurate cut/paste of the raw markdown source, toggle to raw first. Document this in the binding's help text.

## Acceptance

- Assistant messages render with at minimum: syntax-highlighted code blocks, formatted tables, styled lists/emphasis
- `R` toggle works in transcript view; default state = rendered
- Toggle preserves scroll position
- Streaming-phase behavior: raw text shown live; transition to rendered (or stay raw if toggle=raw) on session.done
- `cargo test --workspace` green
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- Charmbracelet's Glamour (Go): https://github.com/charmbracelet/glamour — the design reference
- termimad (Rust): https://github.com/Canop/termimad
- pulldown-cmark (Rust): https://github.com/raphlinus/pulldown-cmark
- Theme coordination: mu-u8a (TUI color polish — moonfly/github_dark family)
- Pattern shelf: mu-zh1 (TUI references including Charmbracelet design language)

## Notes

Filed 2026-05-13 after Charmbracelet survey. Updated 2026-05-14 with raw/rendered toggle requirement after operator UX feedback. Concrete adopt-the-crate task, smaller-scope than full Glamour reimplementation. Sibling to mu-di4 (throbber).

title: Thread system_prompt from CreateSessionRequest to Provider::stream
description:
## Why

`CreateSessionRequest.system_prompt: Option<String>` exists at the wire protocol layer (crates/mu-core/src/protocol.rs:66) but is **accepted at the API boundary and silently dropped**. The Provider::stream trait signature only takes (messages, tools); no system prompt parameter. So clients can send the field expecting customization and get no effect.

Surfaced 2026-05-11 while implementing mu-i6j (cache_control markers on anthropic provider request). The implementation comments explicitly note this gap:

> System prompt is the other classic cache target but mu doesn't currently thread `system_prompt` from CreateSessionRequest through to Provider::stream — see protocol.rs:66 for the wire field; the body construction does not include a `system` key. When that gap is closed, add a second cache_control marker on the last system content block.

See commit ykumwozs 77bb0ce4.

## What

1. Plumb `system_prompt: Option<String>` from the session manager into AgentLoop / Provider invocations. Existing call sites: dispatch::handle_create_session, sessions storage, agent loop's call-out to the provider.
2. Extend Provider::stream trait signature with system_prompt parameter, OR store it on the AgentConfig (preferred — keeps the trait stable and lets each provider decide how to render it).
3. In anthropic provider's build_request_body: if system_prompt is Some, include a top-level `system` field with a content-block array containing the prompt + cache_control marker (mirroring how tools are cached in mu-i6j).
4. Other providers (openai_codex, openrouter, faux) get equivalent treatment.

## Acceptance

- A session created with system_prompt: Some("...") sees the prompt reach the LLM (verifiable by asking 'what's your system prompt' and seeing the contents).
- For anthropic_api, the F5 dashboard's cache% column rises further (system prompt joins tools as a cached prefix).
- Workspace tests still pass; new tests assert the wire-level translation.

## Out of scope

- Designing what mu's default system prompt should be. (Today's behavior is "no system prompt"; this bead preserves that as the default by treating None == today's behavior.)
close_reason: done

title: Skills and tool schemas as rope spans (eliminate parallel loading mechanisms)
description:
Refactor skill activation and tool registration to emit spans into the rope rather than maintain separate loaded-state structures.

## Scope

In: SkillManager emits skill.activated { skill_id, span_refs } events; ToolRegistry represents each tool's schema as an addressable span; capability attenuation becomes a pointer-set filter; RetainedRope API gains activate_skill / deactivate_skill / filter_tools / provenance.

Out: span granularity policy (start per-file, refine later); skill activation triggers (keep current); operator-view UI changes (handled in mu-NEW-4).

## Why

Today skill loading and tool registration are separate code paths from context management. Unifying them: activation/deactivation IS pointer-set membership change. Subagent capability filtering reuses existing rope machinery; provenance built in.

## Acceptance

Skill-activation behavior preserved; tool schemas render identically at agent-view; capability attenuation produces observable pointer-set diff; provenance lookup works; tests for activate/deactivate, filtered tool sets, span provenance; workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-3-skills-tools-as-spans.md
Blocked-by: mu-qk8, mu-ktq
close_reason: Merged in PR #13 as c8a307f

title: mu-core: split agent/loop_.rs into orchestrator + extracted state machines (invoke / execute_tools / autonomy / compaction_integration)
description:
## Problem

`crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs` is the file where everything meets:

- queue / action handling
- message history management
- provider streaming integration
- tool execution
- approval waiting (input_required)
- retry policy
- capability checks
- provider status ticks
- autonomous mode
- compaction integration
- context assembly events
- cancellation semantics

The pure-planner extraction already underway is the right direction.
The loop should become an orchestrator around smaller, testable state
machines rather than the place where everything meets.

Heavy in-file comments are currently doing invariant-documentation
work that should be code structure (the dense comments are necessary
because the control flow is too dense; smaller modules with typed
interfaces would let the structure speak for itself).

## Proposal

Refactor `agent/loop_.rs` into a thin orchestrator with extracted
modules:

```text
crates/mu-core/src/agent/
  loop_.rs                 // orchestrator only: top-level state, action queue
  invoke.rs                // provider invocation + streaming integration
  execute_tools.rs         // tool dispatch, approval waiting, retry policy
  autonomy.rs              // autonomous-mode wakeup + decision logic
  compaction_integration.rs // when/how to trigger compaction
  planners.rs              // already-extracted planner code centralized
  tool_history.rs          // tool call/result tracking and provenance
```

Each extracted module should have a small, well-typed surface;
unit-testable in isolation; document its invariants explicitly.

## Acceptance

- loop_.rs is significantly smaller, focused on orchestration
- Each extracted module has unit tests for its state machine
- Existing agent-loop integration tests still pass
- `cargo clippy -D warnings` clean
- Dense control-flow comments in loop_.rs get replaced by smaller,
  focused modules where the structure documents itself

## Why this matters

Biggest risk in agent-edited codebases is gravity-well files where
local reasoning becomes impossible. agent/loop_.rs is currently the
worst offender. Splitting it makes future agent-assisted modifications
safer because changes can be reasoned about within smaller, typed
boundaries.

## Cross-refs

- AGENTS.md "no 27k-line files" rule
- gpt-5.5 code review of mu, daemon f3845422 session-4 (2026-05-15)
- Related modularization: protocol.rs split + dispatch.rs split (filed
  same session)
- Touches future work: mu-kgu compaction beads, autonomy beads
close_reason: Merged in PR #65 — loop_.rs split into 5 cohesive modules, mu-wk2 integrated, 363+ tests pass, gate-validated ## Files block

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Additional refactor item from gpt code review (mu daemon f3845422 session-4, 2026-05-16): introduce explicit typed state structs for per-ask state and per-session state. Currently agent/loop_.rs uses ad-hoc state passing across the orchestration loop. After the file split lands, define typed structs that make the state-passing invariants explicit at the type level rather than at the comment level. This is gpt's highest-value-cleanup item #7 and is tightly coupled to this split — not worth a separate bead, just include in scope when this bead is picked up.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
**Recovery candidate: jj change vnmssvsw (worktree-mu-nk3-split@origin)** — surfaced 2026-05-16 during jj loose-head audit.

The real refactor work exists here, distinct from the fictional kolvssqm/PR #52 commit that operator closed. Same Haiku 4.5 worker produced both — one actually did the split, the other hallucinated it. Only the fictional one got submitted.

Actual contents of vnmssvsw (6 file changes, 586 insertions, 863 deletions):
- crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/autonomy.rs (+27 LOC, new)
- crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/compaction_integration.rs (+12 LOC, new)
- crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/execute_tools.rs (+377 LOC, new)
- crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/invoke.rs (+146 LOC, new)
- crates/mu-core/src/agent/{loop_.rs => loop_/mod.rs} (trimmed -887 LOC)
- crates/mu-core/src/agent/{loop_tests.rs => loop_/tests.rs} (rename only)

Current main.loop_.rs is 2059 LOC (vnmssvsw's pre-refactor baseline was 2038, so only ~21 LOC of subsequent drift — rebase should be mostly mechanical).

**Recovery options:**

1. **Rebase + new PR.** `jj rebase -r vnmssvsw -d main`, resolve any 21-LOC drift, run `cargo test --workspace && cargo clippy -D warnings && cargo fmt --check`, push fresh PR. Lowest-effort path if the rebase is clean.

2. **Use vnmssvsw as reference, re-do.** Treat vnmssvsw as a worked example, redo the split by hand on current main. Slower but guarantees the result is reviewed against the new mu-b5kl verification gate (which would have caught the fictional kolvssqm submission).

Caveat for either path: the same worker fingerprint produced both real and fictional commits. Review the actual diff carefully — don't trust the commit description by itself. Run `jj show vnmssvsw --stat` and `jj show vnmssvsw` to inspect before promoting.

**Cross-refs:**
- mu-b5kl (new bead) — worker verification gate that would have caught the fictional submission
- jj loose-head audit script: ~/.local/bin/jj-orphan-audit

title: mu-7rk-g: Multi-step auth exchange state registry (defer until 2nd mechanism)
description:
**mu-7rk decomposition phase G — DEFERRED.** Multi-step auth exchange state registry. Only needed once a 2nd auth mechanism (GSSAPI, SASL OAUTHBEARER, etc.) lands. Filed now so the design isn't lost.

## Why

Codex important #5 on original mu-7rk:

> "peer.auth_response has no in-flight challenge-state registry. It loops over all handlers and asks each to process an arbitrary server_state_id. This is unsafe for future multi-step mechanisms: state IDs are not bound to a mechanism, connection, expiry, or nonce."

BEARER (v1) is single-step — `step_initial` always returns `Done` or `Denied`. The `Challenge` outcome and `peer.auth_response` RPC exist as scaffolding for multi-step mechanisms but have no state machine yet. This bead adds the state machine **when needed**.

**Defer triggers:** any bead proposing GSSAPI / SASL OAUTHBEARER / TLS-cert mechanism implementation MUST land mu-7rk-g first.

## Scope

### In

- Per-connection pending-auth state registry:
  - Keyed by **cryptographically-random `server_state_id`** (e.g., 128-bit, generated via `rand::thread_rng()` over `getrandom`)
  - Stores: `mechanism: AuthMechanism`, `handler_state: Box<dyn AuthHandlerState>`, `created_at: Instant`, `single_use: bool`
  - Single-use semantics enforced by removal-on-lookup (`take`, not `get`)
  - Expiry via `created_at + AUTH_CHALLENGE_TTL` (default 30s); expired entries garbage-collected on next access OR via background task
- `AuthMechanismHandler` trait extension: `step_response` receives the `handler_state` from the registry, NOT just the wire `server_state_id`
- `peer.auth_response` dispatch handler:
  - Looks up state by `server_state_id`
  - Reject if: not found, expired, mechanism mismatch (state was for mechanism X, response claims mechanism Y), connection mismatch (state created on connection A, response on connection B)
  - All rejections return `Denied{MalformedExchange}`
- Tests:
  - Multi-step happy path (synthetic test handler that does `Challenge → Done`)
  - Replay attack: same `server_state_id` used twice → 2nd rejected
  - Cross-mechanism: state for mechanism X, response claims mechanism Y → rejected
  - Cross-connection: state created on A, response on B → rejected (depends on connection identity being trackable)
  - Expiry: state created, wait 31s, response → rejected as expired

### Out

- Implementing GSSAPI / SASL OAUTHBEARER / TLS-cert handlers (each is its own bead)
- Persistence of pending-auth state across daemon restarts (in-memory only; restart invalidates)

## Acceptance

- All five test cases above pass
- `cargo test --workspace` green; BEARER (single-step) flow continues to work without entering the registry
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean
- New workspace dep candidates: `rand` (likely already in tree); `getrandom` (likely already in tree). Verify both before adding.
- Audit: grep that `peer.auth_response` cannot succeed without going through the state registry lookup

## References

- Codex review: `~/.claude-personal/experiments/codex-review-mu-7rk-output.txt`
  - important #5 (multi-step state registry)
  - design_concern #3 ("future multi-step design is incomplete")
- Provenance: deferred from original mu-7rk because no 2nd mechanism is in flight; filed in decomp to prevent loss
- Composes with mu-7rk-c (gate) — the registry only matters for protected RPCs the gate enforces

title: Implement mu-037 phase 2: cross-daemon unix-socket peer.hello

title: TUI: suppress empty 'assistant' block on tool-only turns
description:
Currently when the model emits a turn that is entirely tool calls (no text content blocks), the transcript renders a 'assistant — (no text in this turn)' block above the magenta tool block. That's cosmetic cruft.

Fix in crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs::render_transcript_lines: when an 'assistant_message_event' has empty joined text AND there's a 'tool_call' or 'tool_result' event in the same logical turn (i.e. next significant event in the log is a tool_call before the next done), skip rendering the empty assistant block entirely. The tool block itself is the visible record of what the model did this turn.

Discovered in mu-7n3 verify screenshot (2026-05-11).

title: mu-tui: archived session restore picker (fzf+rg+jq enhancement on mu-770)
description:
## Problem

mu-770 covers archive/restore mechanics (session.remove, archive, restore,
list_archived RPCs + TUI affordances). The UX for restoring an archived
session is open — could be a basic list, or richer picker.

## Proposal

Implement restore-picker as fzf-style fuzzy-search popup with rg + jq
integration for filtering/preview:

- Open via shortcut from F3→F3 (e.g., `:restore` or key binding)
- Fuzzy-search across archived session names, last prompts, dates, models
- Live preview of highlighted archived session's last few turns
- Enter to restore, Esc to cancel

Builds on whatever basic restore UI mu-770 lands. If mu-770's TUI scope
already includes this, treat as a follow-up enhancement.

## Acceptance

- Picker opens on key/command from F3 session list
- Type-to-filter narrows the list
- Preview pane shows enough to identify the right session
- Restore action moves session back to active list

## Cross-refs

title: Agent-view vs operator-view projections from the rope
description:
Introduce ProjectionTarget enum (AgentView | OperatorView) and two distinct rendering paths from the same retained pointer set. ProviderRenderer takes a target.

## Scope

In: ProjectionTarget enum; OperatorViewRenderer trait; per-span-kind operator-view rules (tool result -> structured table; skill activation -> one-line badge; tool schema -> name; memory injection -> collapsible); TUI changes (mu-pex / mu-jb3) consume operator-view rendering; tests for both targets.

Out: drill-down UI for inspecting agent-view at specific turn; operator-view variations (compact/detailed/debug) — start with one canonical view.

## Why

LLM tools conflate agent view and operator view. Humans and models have different attention budgets and relevance criteria. Separating projections: operator gets usable surface (skimmable tool results, collapsible context); agent gets full fidelity; debugging becomes possible (AgentView at turn N).

## Acceptance

AgentView produces output identical to current agent context rendering; OperatorView produces distinct usable rendering; TUI integrates (at least mu-pex system_prompt indicator sources operator-view); provenance preserved for span-click drill-down; workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-4-projection-targets.md
Blocked-by: mu-ktq
Existing TUI: mu-pex, mu-62s, mu-jb3, mu-n48
close_reason: Landed in xszxyzyy 8920297c — per-SpanKind OperatorView rules: name/badge for tool/skill, collapsible for memory, summary for tool-result/compaction, byte-count for file-load. AgentView verbatim. Acceptance tests: AgentView unchanged for original 4, AgentView != OperatorView for 6 enriched kinds (>= 4 required). cargo test --workspace green; mu-core 136 → 141. mu-ovl closes; mu-ovl-tui follow-on bead filed separately.

description:
Support Claude Code natively as an agent backend/pump in addition to the direct Anthropic API provider. Treat Claude Code as an agent backend with its own session state, tools, MCP config, permissions, and subscription-backed execution path — not as a normal completion provider.

Candidate pump implementations:
- claude -p --resume turn-based wakeups
- persistent stream-json subprocess bridge if multi-turn works

The bridge participates in mu's mailbox/event system and externally pumps Claude Code turns when messages/events arrive.

title: Add mu release binary to stable PATH (or symlink) for agent-router codex backend
description:
## Why

Overnight session tried to delegate mu-036 spec analysis to GPT via `agent-router --auth codex-oauth` (per the routing policy that says 'lean on GPT for spec-heavy tasks'). The router's codex backend expects `mu` to be on PATH for its mu_client integration. mu's release binary lives at `~/src/public_github/mu/target/release/mu` but isn't symlinked or PATH-exposed.

Prepending PATH inline (`PATH="$HOME/src/public_github/mu/target/release:$PATH" agent-router ...`) works one-shot but doesn't survive the next shell or programmatic invocation from a script that doesn't think to set PATH.

## What

Two reasonable options:
1. Symlink the release binary: `ln -sf ~/src/public_github/mu/target/release/mu ~/.local/bin/mu`. Lives alongside claude-code at `~/.local/bin` (per memory `reference_claude_binary.md` pattern). Risk: rebuilding the workspace overwrites the binary in-place, so the symlink stays valid — but a `cargo clean` breaks it. Acceptable; rerunning `cargo build --release` restores it.
2. Add the path to zsh PATH via `~/.zsh/zenvironment` (the canonical PATH-setup source per the secrets-config layout we already use). Always works; doesn't need a re-symlink after `cargo clean`.

I'd go with (2) — it's the same pattern `tq` and other build-time-installed binaries use already.

## Acceptance

`mu serve --help` works from a fresh terminal without PATH manipulation. `agent-router --auth codex-oauth ...` works without the user prepending PATH. The session-router falls back gracefully (already does — errors say 'No such file or directory: mu') if the binary is genuinely missing.
close_reason: Already done — ~/.local/bin/mu is a symlink to ~/src/public_github/mu/target/release/mu. Confirmed 2026-05-15 during dot-claude-personal gitsah setup work. Use case (agent-router codex backend finding mu on PATH) is now operational.

title: Metrics framework: per-call time decomposition + quality proxies
description:
Design + implementation arc for mu's ongoing observability. Came out of refinement-experiment discussion 2026-05-11: per-request hard metrics vs per-request quality metrics are different beasts, and we should be deliberate about which we track and why.

Captured in commit/results doc: refinement arm 0/3 forbidden vs baseline 3/3, at 2.2x wall time. That cost is fine for delegation-style work but expensive for interactive — we want to be able to SEE the cost decomposition (TTFT, streaming, tool time, thinking) rather than just total wall.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Two classes of metrics, very different to track
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Hard metrics (always trackable, no ground truth):
  - TTFT = AwaitingFirstToken → Streaming transition (mu-035)
  - Streaming duration
  - ToolExecuting duration (sum)
  - Wall end-to-end
  - Tokens in/out, cache hit %, tool count
  - Per-provider error rate

These don't tell us correctness but they DO catch drift: median TTFT per provider per hour would catch 'codex is stalled' in minutes instead of the 14h silence we had on 2026-05-10/11.

Quality metrics (need fixture):
  - Success rate vs ground truth
  - Refinement passes to clean
  - Cost-to-correct = total tokens to 1 successful response

These need a benchmark fixture (like the spec-summary task in protocol-eval). Outside fixtures, every request is unique.

Proxy metrics (work without ground truth):
  - Refinement-fire rate (if we run grader on everything)
  - User-marked retry rate (TUI accept/reject affordance)
  - Tool error rate (model-induced vs system-induced)

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Implementation arc
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Now-easy (post mu-035 A/B/C + mu-038):
  - daemon.usage_history projection RPC returning median/p95 of
    TTFT / streaming_duration / tool_duration / wall, grouped by
    (provider, model, time-bucket).
  - Backed by aggregating ProviderStatus events from the event log
    (or live tracker snapshots once tracker is in Sessions — Phase D).

Soonish (mu-xln existing P2 bead — F5 Usage Dashboard):
  - Renders the daemon.usage_history projection.
  - Per-provider/model rows with TTFT p95, streaming duration p95,
    tool-call-count distribution.
  - Cost estimate column once we have a per-provider price table.

Later (quality proxies):
  - TUI affordance: '👍 helpful' / '👎 not what I wanted' / 'try again'
    after each session.done.
  - Capture in event log (new EventPayload variant: UserFeedback).
  - Surface as a quality-proxy column in F5.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Cross-references
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Depends on:
  - mu-iwq Phase D (daemon.outstanding_calls + ProviderStatusTracker
    in Sessions) — for snapshot enumeration. Phase A/B/C already
    give us the per-call event stream.
  - mu-038 projection-query patterns — same code area, same style.

Composes with:
  - mu-xln (F5 dashboard) — its data source.
  - mu-036 autonomous loop — refinement-fire-rate is a great
    quality proxy for autonomous sessions.

Not in scope here:
  - Cost mapping (provider+model → $/1M tokens). Should be its
    own small bead because it's an updated-rarely lookup table.

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

When to implement: not blocking daily-driver. Worth picking up
after mu-iwq Phase D lands or as a parallel slice if a slow
provider catches our attention again.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Per-model corrections rate is a great proxy metric to add to the design — break down refinement_fire_rate by (provider, model) and you get a comparative quality fingerprint. Different models have different priors (today's experiment confirmed gpt-5.5 has a strong login/logout prior; haiku-4.5 may have different ones). Tracking corrections_per_call by model surfaces:
  - which models are best for which task types (factual extraction, code review, etc.)
  - whether a model is degrading over time (vendor pushed an update? prior drift?)
  - cost-of-correctness tradeoff (cheaper-but-more-corrections vs more-expensive-but-fewer)

Add to the F5 dashboard sketch: per-(provider, model) row showing corrections_per_100_calls when grader was enabled. Surfaces 'haiku gets 5/100 wrong on summarisation; gpt-5.5 gets 33/100; sonnet gets 2/100' or whatever the truth turns out to be.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
Phase 1 (A-scope) landed in commit rwyyuout 28c677c6 (`feat(mu-pex): daemon.usage_history projection RPC — Phase 1 (A-scope)`).

What landed:
- New RPC `daemon.usage_history` with (since_unix_ms?, until_unix_ms?, time_bucket_ms?) request params, returns rows grouped by (provider, model, time-bucket).
- Per-row distributions (median+p95): tool_total_ms, wall_ms, model_call_latency_ms (proxy: AssistantMessageEvent.ts − ContextAssembly.ts).
- Per-row sums: input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, reasoning_tokens, tool_call_count.
- Per-row counts: session_count, error_count.
- Aggregates over in-memory sessions visible via `sessions.snapshot_for_listing()`.
- 12 new unit tests; full workspace test suite green (289 passed, 1 ignored).

Explicitly None in Phase 1 (will land in Phase 1.5 / Iteration 2):
- `ttft_ms` — needs durable `EventPayload::ProviderStatusUpdate` (currently wire-only mu-035).
- `streaming_ms` — same reason.

Phase 1.5 plan (queued in task list):
1. Add `EventPayload::ProviderStatusUpdate` variant (mu-core/src/event_log.rs).
2. Extend `forwarder.rs::to_log_event` to map `AgentEvent::ProviderStatus` → that variant alongside the existing wire-side mapping.
3. Update `payload_kind_str` in dispatch.rs and the TUI's sidechannel branch in render_transcript_lines.
4. Extend `usage_history::extract_per_session_metrics` to compute TTFT (AwaitingFirstToken→Streaming transition) and streaming_ms (time spent in Streaming per call).
5. Populate the two Option<PercentileStats> fields in UsageHistoryRow.

Still out of scope at the bead level:
- mu-xln (F5 dashboard) — UI consumer of this RPC.
- UserFeedback EventPayload + TUI affordance.
- Cost mapping (provider+model → $/1M tokens) — its own follow-up bead.
- Cross-daemon and disk-only historical coverage — gated on mu-iwq Phase D + mu-935 FileBackend.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
Phase 1.5 landed in commit lrslqwuv b0944d40 — durable ProviderStatusUpdate + TTFT/streaming populated.

Changes:
- New `EventPayload::ProviderStatusUpdate` variant (mu-core/src/event_log.rs) mirroring the wire-side `ProviderStatusEvent` minus session_id.
- forwarder `to_log_event` now maps `AgentEvent::ProviderStatus` → that variant. Wire-side `translate_event` unchanged: both projections still derive from the same AgentEvent source — they no longer disagree about durability.
- payload_kind_str (dispatch) + derive_status_from_events (discovery) + render_transcript_lines sidechannel (mu-tui) all extended to handle the new variant.
- `usage_history::extract_per_session_metrics` now walks ProviderStatusUpdate events; consecutive transition emissions (elapsed_ms == 0) yield duration samples for the prior state. Periodic ticks (elapsed_ms > 0) are skipped — they share started_at with their opening transition.
- `aggregate_into_rows` populates `row.ttft_ms` and `row.streaming_ms` via `percentile_stats` over pooled samples. Both stay None when a group has no samples — same shape as Phase 1.

Tests added (+6 over Phase 1, total now 295 active / 1 ignored):
- extraction_computes_ttft_from_awaiting_first_token_transition
- extraction_periodic_ticks_do_not_close_a_state_period
- extraction_records_streaming_periods_separately (interrupted Streaming yields 2 samples)
- extraction_unterminated_state_period_is_dropped (in-flight or aborted)
- aggregation_populates_ttft_and_streaming_in_row
- forwarder::log_records_provider_status_update

Still queued (parking-lot, not in any commit):
- mu-xln (F5 dashboard) — UI consumer of daemon.usage_history.
- TUI feedback affordance (👍/👎/try again) + `UserFeedback` EventPayload variant — quality proxy.
- Cost mapping (provider+model → $/1M tokens) — its own follow-up bead (out-of-scope per the original bead).
- Cross-daemon / disk-only historical coverage — gated on mu-iwq Phase D + mu-935 FileBackend.
- Per-comment from 2026-05-11 20:14: per-(provider, model) corrections_per_100_calls when grader enabled — needs refinement integration first.

comments.author: claude
comments.text:
Phase 1.5 follow-up fix in commit sunvrnkp 2b84e8b1 — discovered via hand-test against anthropic-api+haiku.

Two bugs in the initial Phase 1.5 transition-detection logic (both in extract_per_session_metrics):

1. **The transition filter `elapsed_ms == 0` was too strict.** The agent loop emits the AwaitingFirstToken→Streaming transition with elapsed_ms set to call_started.elapsed() (loop_.rs:880), not 0. The filter silently dropped that emission. Net effect: `ttft_ms.count` was counting gaps *between asks* (the only AFT→AFT pair the filter saw); `streaming_ms.count` was always 0.

2. **No Done-driven close-out.** The agent loop doesn't emit a Streaming→Idle transition on session.done. One ask's open Streaming state would bleed into the inter-ask gap before the next AFT entry, polluting streaming_ms with wait time.

Fix: identify transitions by started_at_unix_ms uniqueness (ticks within a period share their period's started_at; transitions always introduce a new one); close out pending state on Done using the Done's timestamp.

Hand-test now produces credible numbers:
- ttft_ms p95 = 1184ms for haiku via anthropic-api (TTFT-dominated, as expected for tiny prompts)
- streaming_ms p95 = 62ms for 5-token responses (~10ms/token)
- wall_ms matches Done.elapsed_ms exactly

Tests added: extraction_streaming_entry_with_nonzero_elapsed_still_counts + extraction_done_closes_out_pending_state_between_asks. Workspace tests: 297 active, 1 ignored.

title: mu-memory: event-pointer index + recall surface, separated from compaction
description:
## Problem

mu has no memory subsystem yet. Sessions accumulate context within their
event log, but there's no first-class concept of "memory" — durable,
queryable references to facts/decisions/patterns that survive across
sessions and inform future ones. This is foundational for the continuity
goal ("you-ness preservation"): a fresh session should be able to recall
relevant prior context without the user re-supplying it every time.

claude-code uses a per-host SQLite (`~/.local/share/agent.sqlite`) for
this. mu can do better by leaning on its own substrate.

## Design

### Memory as event-pointer index

Memory entries are typed pointers into the existing event log, not a
parallel data store. A memory entry says "event N (or event-range N..M)
in session S is relevant for purpose P." This means:

- No duplication of content (the events ARE the content)
- Provenance is intrinsic (you can always walk back to the source event)
- Privacy/redaction model is unified (cull the event → memory points to a
  tombstone or relocated copy)

### Memories as events (recursive / self-describing)

A new memory entry is itself emittable as an event in the log. The act of
remembering is auditable, and memory creation/update is a first-class
event-stream operation — no separate write path.

### Relocation on event-log cull

When old audits/event-logs are culled (compaction, age-out, retention
policy), still-used memory entries get relocated:

- The pointed-at event content is copied into the "current" log location
- An origin-appendage records where it came from
- Multiple relocations over time accumulate a chain of appendages
- Memory pointers update to the new location atomically with the cull

Memory survives event-log lifecycle changes without losing provenance.
Enterprise retention (90-day cull, GDPR right-to-be-forgotten, etc.)
becomes tractable without breaking memory.

### Recall (retrieval surface)

Distinct layer over the memory index:

- Query: by tag, type, session, time-range, free-text, embedding-similarity
- Score/rank: relevance ranking with policy-driven weights
- ACL filter: capability check before returning matches (ties into
  companion discovery+permissions bead filed same session)
- Surface as both: a tool agents can invoke, and a TUI affordance

### Separated from compaction

Memory storage and event-log compaction are explicitly separate concerns
with separate policies. This means we can change our minds independently
on: aggressiveness of compaction, retention shape of memory, whether
enterprise policies override either. Compaction is mu-kgu's domain;
memory + recall is this bead's.

## Acceptance

- Memory entries can be created (as events), referenced (by pointer-walk),
  recalled (by query)
- Recall surface exists as both an agent-callable tool and a TUI affordance
- Event-log cull preserves memory pointers via relocation + origin-chain
- Memory policy and compaction policy configurable independently
- Capability checks gate recall results

## Cross-refs

- mu-kgu (CompactionPolicy trait — explicitly separated concern)
- mu-cha (F2 session tree — recall could surface there)
- mu-ib5 (F9 mailbox view — mailboxes are an event channel memory may
  want to point into)
- mu-7rk-* (capability decomposition — provides ACL substrate)
- memory 03b44633 (capability broker pattern)
- Companion to discovery+permissions bead (filed same session)
- Compares to claude-code's `~/.local/share/agent.sqlite` (different
  substrate; mu's event-pointer design is intrinsically auditable)
- Provenance: user feedback session 2026-05-15

title: mu-tui: per-session control surface (model selection + command palette + picker)
description:
## Problem

Sessions on mu currently can't have their model changed mid-session, and
mu doesn't yet have an analog of claude-code's slash commands operating
per-session. If a session is created with the wrong model, it sits unusable
until the program quits.

## Proposal

Add a per-session command surface analogous to claude-code's slash commands,
but operating against the selected session rather than globally:

- `/model <name>` — change the session's model (requires model picker UX)
- `/provider <name>` — switch underlying provider on the session
- `/goal <text>` — fire a goal-protocol-style autonomous run
- `/status` — show this session's current state
- `/rewind <n>` — undo last N turns
- `/rename <text>` — give session a friendly name (surfaces in F3 list)
- room for mu-specific commands beyond what claude-code has

Includes a model picker (list + autocomplete + favorites/most-frequent-first)
since `/model` needs an interactive selector.

## Acceptance

- Selected session in F3 exposes a command palette (`:` prefix or slash)
- Commands apply only to the selected session, not global state
- `/model` switching produces fresh provider context without restarting mu
- Model picker shows favorites first; type-to-filter

## Cross-refs

- F3 session-list summary bead (`/rename` surfaces there)
- Provenance: TUI feedback (mu daemon f3845422 session-2, 2026-05-15)

title: Architecture: amend event-sourced-context.md with skill/tool/cache/projection/subagent/file-watch extensions
providers hardcode max_tokens=4096 regardless of model — truncates Opus/Sonnet responses
description:
Both Anthropic and OpenRouter providers in mu hardcode `\"max_tokens\": 4096` on every request, regardless of model. Opus 4.7 supports up to 32768 output tokens; Haiku 4.5 supports up to 8192. Truncating every response at 4096 wastes the available budget and causes visible cut-offs.

## Code locations

- `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs:204` — `\"max_tokens\": 4096`
- `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openrouter.rs:200` — `\"max_tokens\": 4096`
- Tests pin to 4096:
  - `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic_tests.rs:45`
  - `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openrouter_tests.rs:119`

## Symptom

Opus 4.7 session 2026-05-14, event log `03b75bc4a9c48f82`: workspace-review response cut off mid-sentence after ~11k characters of structured analysis. Final turn's API stop_reason was `\"max_tokens\"` (confirming the cap was hit, not natural completion).

Haiku session earlier same day exhibited the same truncation shape on a similar prompt.

## Fix options (lean toward B)

### A. Bump the constant

Change 4096 → 16384 globally. Cheap, one-line. Downside: Haiku gets a larger budget than its native max (which would be clamped to 8192 server-side anyway, but it's sloppy).

### B. Model-aware max_tokens lookup (recommended)

Add a small helper `max_tokens_for_model(model: &str) -> u32`:
- haiku-4-5* → 8192
- sonnet-4-6* → 8192 (verify)
- opus-4-7* → 16384 (or 32768 for max use of Opus's capacity)
- unknown → 4096 (current default; safe fallback)

Used by both Anthropic and OpenRouter providers. Lives near the model-name normalization code if it exists, else its own module.

### C. Per-session config override

Operator-set max_tokens per session via `:max_tokens N` colon-command or RPC. Composes with B as a per-session override of the lookup default. Defer to follow-on bead unless trivial.

## Scope

IN:
- Implement option B: model-aware max_tokens lookup.
- Both providers use the helper.
- Tests updated; new test asserts opus model gets > 4096; new test asserts unknown model still gets safe fallback.

OUT:
- Per-session override (option C) — separate bead if wanted.
- Auto-continuation on truncation — separate decision.
- TUI \"response truncated\" badge — depends on mu-s5h fix first.

## Acceptance

- `max_tokens_for_model` helper exists, tested.
- Anthropic + OpenRouter providers use it.
- Existing tests updated to reflect the new defaults for their fixture models.
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- Anthropic API max_tokens docs: model-specific maxima (Opus 4.7 = 32768 in current API; Haiku 4.5 = 8192)
- Event log showing real truncation: `/home/tcovert/.local/share/mu/events/03b75bc4a9c48f82/session-1.jsonl`
- Composes with mu-s5h: the stop_reason aggregation bug; once both land, operator can both see WHY the response stopped AND have enough budget for it not to stop prematurely
close_reason: Merged in PR #11 as d0acadf

title: mu-mailbox-correlation: add correlation_id + reply_to_session_id to mailbox wire shape (orchestrator task↔result matching)
description:
Add `correlation_id` and `reply_to_session_id` to mailbox post/list/event wire shapes so orchestrator/worker task↔result matching doesn't require encoding correlation in the `body` field by convention.

## Use case

An orchestrator session O posts tasks to workers W₁..Wₙ via `mailbox.post`. Workers process and post results back via `mailbox.post` against O's session_id. Today O has no formal way to match a posted result back to the originating task — it has to encode a correlation ID inside `body` and parse it on the way back. That's:
- Fragile (depends on every worker honoring the convention)
- Untyped (no schema-level guarantee)
- Hard to filter (`mailbox.list` can't return "results for task X" without body inspection)

Formalizing the correlation fields lets `mailbox.list` filter by them and lets the wire schema document the pattern.

## Scope

### In

- Add `correlation_id: Option<String>` and `reply_to_session_id: Option<String>` to:
  - `MailboxPostRequest`
  - `MailboxMessageView` (returned by `mailbox.list`)
  - `MailboxMessageEvent` (the wire notification)
  - `EventPayload::MailboxMessagePosted` event-log variant
- Optional filter: `MailboxListRequest.correlation_id: Option<String>` — when set, return only messages with that correlation_id. (Lets O do `mailbox.list { session_id: O, correlation_id: "task-42" }` to get just the results for one task.)
- Convention: a posted result message sets `correlation_id` to the task's correlation_id and `reply_to_session_id` to the task's `from.session_id`. Neither is enforced; both are present in the wire schema.

### Out

- Auto-matching at the dispatch layer ("post a result and the daemon notifies the original poster"). v1 leaves matching to the consumer of `mailbox.list`.
- TTL on outstanding correlations.
- A new RPC like `mailbox.await_result(correlation_id, timeout_ms)` — composes from `mailbox.list` + polling for now.

## Acceptance

- Wire shape additions compile and serialize as documented.
- `mailbox.post` with `correlation_id` round-trips through `mailbox.list`.
- Filter test: post 3 messages with different correlation_ids, list filtered by one → only that one returned.
- Existing mu-lho tests (l1/l2/l3 in `mailbox_smoke.rs`) still pass — fields are additive and optional.

## Composition

- Builds on mu-lho (closed). Doesn't change mu-lho's authorization, lifecycle, or event-sourcing model — purely additive wire fields.
- Composes with the "supervisor capability axis" follow-up bead: that bead lets O receive notifications for all supervised workers' posts; this bead lets O distinguish "this post is a result of task X."

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-14 / 2026-05-15 during mu-lho orchestrator use-case exploration. Operator: "if I didn't push the use cases it'd be harder to come up with concrete things that we should or should not have."

title: mu-ai: Anthropic provider EOF-without-message_stop should surface as degraded signal, not silent Done
description:
## Problem

In `mu-ai`'s Anthropic provider SSE parsing, if the stream ends WITHOUT
a `message_stop` event (connection drop mid-stream, upstream truncation
without clean terminal), the parser emits `Done` — same event as a
clean message_stop. Resilient but silent.

Operationally, provider truncation / connection drops are then
indistinguishable from clean completions in downstream consumers
(agent loop, event log, TUI). Truncations may be visible elsewhere
(incomplete tool_call inputs caught by the JSON parser) but the
provider-layer signal is lost.

## Proposal

Distinguish "normal message_stop" from "EOF without message_stop" at
the provider event level. Options:

(a) Add a variant/flag to Done: `Done { reason: StopReason }` where
    StopReason is `MessageStop` (clean) or `UnexpectedEof` (degraded)
(b) Emit a separate `Callout` of category "warning" when EOF arrives
    without message_stop, before the Done

Downstream consumers can choose to treat them the same OR surface the
degraded case (callout in TUI, event-log annotation, retry policy).

## Acceptance

- Provider trait surface distinguishes clean-stop from EOF-without-stop
- Anthropic provider emits the correct variant in each case
- Unit test in mu-ai covers the EOF-without-message_stop path
- Existing Done consumers continue to work; signal is available when
  they want it

## Why this matters

mu's design philosophy is "observability as product feature." Silent
degradation contradicts that. This change surfaces a real-world
condition the system currently masks.

## Cross-refs

- Provenance: gpt-5.5 code review of mu, daemon f3845422 session-4
  (2026-05-15)
- mu-pex (metrics framework — this signal feeds in naturally)
close_reason: Merged in PR #49 (1e0748a) — Anthropic provider DegradedEof signal

title: specs/measurements/: collect calibrations (mu-kgu.9 et al) — methodology + numbers + reproduce instructions
description:
## Goal

A single canonical home for benchmarks + calibrations. mu-kgu.9 (tokenizer calibration vs Anthropic at 124k true tokens) is the template. Future opus runs that produce headline numbers should land here, not in scattered MORNING_*.md notes.

## Initial contents (proposed)

- specs/measurements/README.md — index + 'how to add a measurement' guide
- specs/measurements/2026-05-14-mu-kgu.9-tokenizer-calibration.md — methodology, raw numbers, reproduce
- specs/measurements/2026-05-14-mu-kgu-compaction-vs-anthropic.md — the 124k→6k in 17ms vs 38s claim with the source data
- (future) capability-delegation latency, mailbox p99, etc.

## Why now

Per opus review event 221: 'The next time someone runs an Opus benchmark, the writeup has a home.' Also: gives the README rewrite (mu-xs4b) a stable URL to link out to for the 'measurement' part of claim→mechanism→measurement.

## Source

opus-4-7 review event 221, item 2 in recommendations list.

title: bare-equivalent boot mode for mu (minimal-context boot for delegates and sub-sessions)
description:
Implements a minimal-context boot mode for mu, equivalent to claude-code 2.1.139's `--bare` flag. Memory `fb5cd5be`.

## Motivation

Today every mu session boots with the full operator default: CLAUDE.md auto-discovery, auto-memory context injection, plugin sync, hooks. Most of this is wrong for delegate / sub-session use — a delegate that exists to answer one well-scoped question doesn't need the operator's accumulated memory or the hook configuration.

claude-code 2.1.139's `--bare` mode is the same idea: a different *initial retention set* (per the rope amendment in mu-qk8). The rope architecture says this should be declarative — a profile property, not a flag.

## Scope

In:
- SessionBootMode { Full | Bare } (or as a BootProfile carrying which spans to load).
- Bare mode skips: CLAUDE.md auto-discovery, auto-memory injection, plugin/hook sync.
- Bare mode keeps: tool registry, capability, transport.
- Plumbing: create_session and delegate_session accept the boot mode; default is Full for explicit user-driven creates, Bare for delegates by default (with override).
- Tests: a bare session has no CLAUDE.md spans and no auto-memory in its initial retained set.

Out:
- Per-source granularity (skip CLAUDE.md but keep hooks, etc.) — start with one coarse switch; refine if usage shows it's needed.
- Operator-view UI for boot-mode selection — assume CLI / RPC param for now.

## Acceptance

* Boot mode selectable via API (create_session + delegate_session).
* Bare-mode session's initial retained span set is observably smaller (test).
* Default for delegate_session is Bare; create_session default stays Full.
* No regression in existing Full-mode behavior.

## References

- Memory: fb5cd5be (claude-code 2.1.139 mapping)
- Architecture: rope amendment in specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md, Cache-boundary alignment section (the --bare interpretation as a different initial retention set).
- Companions: mu-ikz (authority tier), mu-hlf (file isolation) — together these three constitute the 2.1.139 profile / authority feature mapping.

title: mu-context-ulimit: soft/hard context caps with judge-mediated raise requests
description:
Exploration bead. Treat context size as a **bounded capability with soft/hard thresholds**, where the agent must request elevation past soft, and an independent **judge** (not the agent) evaluates whether elevation is warranted.

## Motivation

Context rot is empirical: model performance degrades well before nominal max context, and the degradation isn't graceful. Anthropic's auto-compaction target of ~150k for Opus reflects this; past that point attention quality drops measurably. Today mu compacts at a threshold (mu-kgu.4) but treats context size as a *budget* managed silently. Reframing it as a *capability with soft and hard caps* surfaces the cost and creates a decision point.

## Design sketch (ulimit-style)

- **soft cap** — per (provider, model) calibrated to the degradation knee (e.g. ~150k Opus, ~80k Sonnet, ~40k Haiku). Hitting soft triggers compaction policy (the mu-kgu integration point).
- **hard cap** — absolute ceiling (nominal max minus safety margin). mu refuses to grow context past hard regardless of agent request.
- **raise request** — between soft and hard, the agent may emit `session.request_context_raise { current_tokens, requested_cap, claimed_reason }`. The supervisor / policy decides.
- **judge-mediated decision** — the request is NOT decided by the agent's self-assessment. Instead, a separate judge call (reusing the mu-kgu.3 HashAndSummaryPolicy + mu-kgu.11 ProviderJudge plumbing) independently evaluates: "given the current rope, is the request justified, or is X tokens droppable instead?"

### Judge verdict shape

```
enum CapRaiseVerdict {
  Approve { recommended_cap: usize, reason: String },
  Deny { recommended_compact: CompactionDirective, reason: String },
  ApproveAndCompact { recommended_cap: usize, also_drop: CompactionDirective, reason: String },
}
```

The third option (compact-and-raise) is the most honest answer when context genuinely contains both load-bearing and droppable spans.

## Composition with existing substrates

- **mu-kgu (compaction substrate)** — soft cap triggers the existing CompactionPolicy. Same machinery. New input: the judge can be asked "is this compactable?" separately from "compact it now."
- **mu-fvy0 (accountable runtime)** — \"raise cap\" is a CapabilityKind axis. Operator can grant blanket raise authority to a session, or require per-request approval. Judge verdict is evidence; capability holder decides.
- **mu-035 (provider_status)** — a new `ProviderStatusKind::JudgeEvaluatingCapRequest` sub-kind so the operator sees the evaluation happening in real time.
- **mu-fvy0-telemetry (mu-5g7i)** — every (request, judge verdict, operator decision, downstream outcome) is a TaskTelemetry datapoint. Over time the analytics layer (mu-8ypx) measures whether judge-approved raises correlate with better task outcomes vs judge-denied + compacted, vs no-request baselines. Empirical loop for tuning the policy.

## Open questions (decide before implementing)

1. **Per-model soft caps**: yes (Opus 4.7 ≠ Sonnet 4.6 ≠ Haiku 4.5 ≠ GPT-5.5). Where do calibrated values live — config file, hardcoded const, telemetry-derived? Probably a config table seeded with vendor defaults, refined from observed telemetry.
2. **Hysteresis**: once raised, when do we downgrade? Auto-downgrade when usage drops under 0.8×soft for N turns? Sticky-for-session? Decide based on telemetry of how often raised sessions stay near the new ceiling vs settle back down.
3. **Judge model selection**: cheap-small (Haiku 4.5, ~$0.005/eval) is ideal — fast, focused, parallel to the main turn. But the judge's accuracy matters for the loop. Calibration question.
4. **Per-request capability vs blanket capability**: per-request is more accountable, blanket is less ceremony. Probably both should be possible, with the default being per-request and operators able to grant blanket per-session.
5. **Failure modes of the judge**: what if the judge itself hallucinates? Falls back to default-deny? Default-approve? The hollow-commit / lying-state classifier (mu-8alb) gives us a way to detect judge errors retrospectively but not prevent them in-flight.

## Acceptance (when this becomes implementable)

- Soft/hard cap fields on session config, per-(provider,model) defaults
- `session.request_context_raise` wire event + policy that intercepts it
- `CapRaiseJudge` consumer of existing JudgeAdapter, with a new prompt schema
- Three verdict types correctly produced and respected
- Telemetry envelope captures the full request→verdict→outcome chain
- Spec'd in `specs/mu-NNN-context-ulimit.md`

## Not implementing now

This is an exploration / vision bead. Implementing requires mu-kgu (done), mu-fvy0-telemetry (mu-5g7i — not yet started), and a config-system pass for per-(provider,model) tables. Worth filing as a coherent target so the substrate pieces are designed with this in mind.

## Provenance

Operator observation at 2026-05-16: "was looking at your context ctx:45%... /compact tries to get under 150k... context rot... should LRU keep at 150k? or let it go to max. Then I thought of ulimit and soft and hard caps." Plus follow-up: "we already have a judge function. That might be a good place for an independent assessment."

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## Refinements 2026-05-16 (operator)

Two structural changes from the initial sketch:

### 1. The raise-request is fire-and-forget from the agent's side

The agent does NOT block waiting for a verdict. It emits `session.request_context_raise` as a signal and continues with whatever context it has. The judge + supervisor process asynchronously. This removes the "synchronous capability gate" complication and matches the mu-kgu.8 background-compaction-worker pattern — the model keeps working, mitigation happens in parallel.

Consequences:
- The agent loop never stalls on a cap request
- The verdict may come AFTER the current turn completes (still useful: informs the NEXT turn's cap)
- The judge can fire background compaction directly when it decides "drop these spans"; the cap-raise flow becomes a unified "context-pressure response" pipeline

### 2. Judge verdicts are recorded, always

Every verdict goes to the event log as a durable EventPayload variant (and into the mu-5g7i telemetry envelope). This is non-negotiable even when nothing acts on the verdict — the recording is what lets the analytics layer (mu-8ypx) measure judge accuracy over time. Without records there is no feedback loop.

### 3. The judge is pluggable

The JudgeAdapter trait from mu-kgu.11 is the extension point. Operators can plug in:
- A cheap-small judge (Haiku 4.5) — default, fast, low-cost
- A same-family judge (Opus 4.7 evaluating its own context) — more expensive, possibly more accurate, possibly more biased
- A domain-specific judge (custom prompt for specific workloads — e.g. preserve all type definitions, drop file contents older than 5 turns)
- A composite judge (multiple judges, vote)
- A null judge (always-approve or always-deny, for testing or specific policies)

Pluggability means experimentation is cheap — operators can A/B different judges and let the telemetry layer compare outcomes.

### Composite picture

```
agent: ...mid-turn, context approaching soft cap...
  -> emit session.request_context_raise (fire-and-forget)
agent: ...continues turn with current context...

(in parallel)
supervisor: receive raise request
  -> invoke pluggable Judge::evaluate(rope, request_envelope)
  -> Judge returns: Approve | Deny | ApproveAndCompact | ScheduleBackgroundCompact
  -> Record verdict in event log + telemetry sink
  -> Act:
     - Approve: raise soft cap for next turn
     - Deny: no action; verdict recorded for analytics
     - ApproveAndCompact: schedule mu-kgu.8 background worker AND raise cap
     - ScheduleBackgroundCompact: dispatch worker, don't raise

agent: ...completes turn, next turn sees new cap and/or compacted rope...
```

The agent never knows it asked. The agent just operates with whatever context shape it has now. The judge's accuracy is empirically measurable retrospectively, which is the only honest way to tune it.

title: aws_recon runner must enforce subprocess timeout
description: Follow-up to PR #29 review. The runner-backed aws_recon path must not rely solely on the sandbox script honoring --call-timeout; mu-coding needs an outer subprocess timeout that kills the child if the runner hangs. Preserve live-recon semantics where call_timeout_secs is per AWS call, so add/derive an overall runner timeout rather than wedging the daemon.
close_reason: completed

title: agent loop hardcodes Done.stop_reason — masks MaxTokens/StopSequence from per-turn stream
description:
The agent loop in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs` always emits `AgentEvent::Done { stop_reason: StopReason::EndTurn, ... }` (or `StopReason::Aborted` for cancellations). It never propagates the provider's per-turn `StopReason` (e.g. `MaxTokens`, `StopSequence`) into the session-level Done event.

## Symptom observed

Opus 4.7 session against mu, 2026-05-14, event log `03b75bc4a9c48f82`:
- Last `assistant_message_event` payload: `stop_reason: "max_tokens"` ← real
- `done` event payload: `stop_reason: "end_turn"` ← misleading projection

The Opus response was cut off mid-sentence (max_tokens cap of 4096 hit). The TUI's "session done" affordance, which reads the Done event, reported it as a clean end_turn — making the truncation invisible to the operator. Same pattern would have appeared with Haiku at the same cap.

## Code locations

All `AgentEvent::Done` emissions in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs`:
- `:645` — iteration cap hit → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`
- `:738` — OutstandingCancelled → `StopReason::Aborted` (correct)
- `:805` — another cancel path → `StopReason::Aborted` (correct)
- `:992` — normal completion → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`
- `:1043` — another completion path → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`
- `:1068` — Outcome::Done helper → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`

The provider returns `AssistantMessage` with a real `stop_reason` (Anthropic provider maps `\"max_tokens\"` → `StopReason::MaxTokens` correctly at `anthropic.rs:344`), but the agent loop discards it.

## Why this matters

Event-sourced context architecture treats projections as derived from the event stream. The `done` event is supposed to reduce per-turn signal into a session-level summary. When the reduction loses information that the source preserved, the projection is wrong. This is exactly the failure mode the architecture is designed to prevent — finding it here means a regression test discipline gap.

Operator impact: a truncated response that looks complete in the TUI. Bigger consequence: any tool/automation downstream of the Done event makes wrong decisions about whether to retry or extend.

## Scope

IN:
- Propagate the final turn's `StopReason` from `assistant_msg.stop_reason` into all `Done` emissions where a turn just completed.
- Hold the latest-known stop_reason in the agent loop's local state, updated each turn.
- For paths where the loop terminates without a final turn (iteration cap, no provider call), keep current behavior (`EndTurn` / `Aborted`) but document why.
- Test: per-turn stop_reason of MaxTokens / StopSequence / EndTurn propagates correctly into the Done event.
- Test: cancellation path still emits Aborted regardless of provider stop_reason.

OUT:
- TUI rendering improvements for the new state (separate bead — operator badge for "response was truncated").
- Auto-continuation policy (\"if max_tokens hit, ask the model to continue\" — separate decision).

## Acceptance

- Done event's stop_reason matches the underlying per-turn provider stop_reason on the natural-completion path.
- New regression tests in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_tests.rs`.
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- Provider stop_reason mapping: `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs:344`
- StopReason enum: `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs`
- Symptom event log: `/home/tcovert/.local/share/mu/events/03b75bc4a9c48f82/session-1.jsonl`
close_reason: Merged in PR #11 as d0acadf

From F3 transcript view, press i and send another prompt to the selected session. Verify:
