title: README rewrite: 'Why this architecture pays off' lead with compaction benchmark (claim → mechanism → measurement)
description:
## Goal

Land a README section that converts the architectural thesis into a concrete cash-out a hostile reader can verify. Right now the README front-loads the substrate (most-demanding read for least-prepared reader); reorder so the proof shows up first.

## Proposed structure (per opus review)

1. One-paragraph what-and-why
2. **NEW: one concrete differentiator with a number** — the compaction story (124k tokens → ~6k in 17ms vs Anthropic's ~38s, ~2200x faster, calibrated against tiktoken cl100k_base; methodology in mu-kgu.9)
3. Quick start
4. What works today (rewrite — brag harder; TUI specifically — 'standalone mu-tui binary with F1-F9 dashboards, transcript, firehose, modal approvals, $EDITOR handoff, ~4500 LOC of polished Ratatui')
5. Architectural bet (current 'Why mu exists' + 'Design principles', moved down)
6. Deep-dive links

The compaction section format = claim → mechanism (where it lives in the code) → measurement (the numbers + how to reproduce).

## Why now

Per opus review event 194 and the followup 221: 'A reader who absorbs the compaction section has absorbed the *shape* of the architecture even though they only read about one feature.' First architectural payoff mature enough to measure — sets the template for auditor / capability / orchestration sections later.

## Acceptance

- README leads with compaction claim + measurement in the first 200 words after the what-and-why
- TUI status line accurately describes mu-tui (drop 'aspirational' framing)
- Architectural thesis moved below the cash-out
- Links out to specs/measurements/ (see follow-up bead)

## Source

opus-4-7 review session 3262c036eaca7daa events 194 + 221.

title: mu-mcp: MCP (Model Context Protocol) plugin/tool-server support
description:
Filed 2026-05-13 after surveying Crush (Charmbracelet's coding agent), which lists MCP support as a first-class feature: 'Extensible: add capabilities via MCPs (http, stdio, and sse)'.

## What MCP is

Model Context Protocol (Anthropic-published spec). A wire protocol for agents to discover and call external tool servers. Three transport flavors: http (network), stdio (subprocess), sse (server-sent events). Decouples tool surface from agent binary.

## Why it'd matter for mu

Today mu's tool surface is built-in: bash, edit, grep, glob, read, write (in crates/mu-coding/src/tools/). Adding a new tool requires modifying the mu binary. MCP would let third-party tools register at session start (specified in config or session.delegate request).

## Relation to existing mu work

- **Capability-broker pattern** (memory 03b44633): MCP would be the WIRE surface; the capability broker is the SECURITY primitive. They compose — MCP tools get the same Capability-typed grants as built-in tools.
- **Skill activation**: mu's skill system already does dynamic capability registration via the rope (SkillActivation SpanKind, mu-ovl). MCP could be one shape of skill: 'this skill activates an MCP server and exposes its tools.'
- **AwsCapability** (mu-f5o): each MCP tool that touches AWS would need an attached AwsCapability for the broker to enforce. Same axis-based attenuation.

## Scope (when picked up)

IN (v1):
- MCP client implementation (probably stdio first — simplest transport, runs the tool server as a subprocess of mu serve)
- Tool registration during session setup
- Routing tool calls through the Tool trait surface mu already has
- Capability enforcement at the tool-dispatch layer (existing infrastructure)

OUT:
- HTTP/SSE transports (later, after stdio works)
- Built-in MCP server registry (operator manages config)
- Authentication / authorization beyond what Capability provides

## Trigger to actually pick this up

When a third-party tool (or a tool you want to write outside mu's tree) is needed. Today everything mu does is in-tree; until that pressure shows up, MCP is overkill.

## References

- MCP spec: https://modelcontextprotocol.io
- Crush's MCP integration: https://github.com/charmbracelet/crush (Go reference)
- Anthropic SDK MCP support: should already work in mu's anthropic provider — just need the client end to call/discover MCP tools
- Pattern shelf: mu-zh1 (Crush as the design reference)
- Security primitive: memory 03b44633 (capability_broker_pattern)

title: queue-boundary timestamp pairs: generalize ProviderStatusUpdate's pattern to all internal queues
description:
Generalize the 'send/receive timestamp pair captures latency that hides in queues' pattern operator described 2026-05-16. mu already does this for the model-call path via ProviderStatusUpdate (AwaitingFirstToken→Streaming gives TTFT, Streaming→done gives streaming_ms). Extend to the other internal queue boundaries.

## Internal queue boundaries today

1. agent_loop → forwarder mpsc channel (events emitted, consumed asynchronously)
2. forwarder → SessionEventLog::append (synchronous but locks)
3. forwarder → NotificationWriter::emit (wire send to JSON-RPC peer)
4. session → mailbox post (mu-lho — local today, cross-daemon later)

None of these have endpoint timestamps recorded. Latency in any of them is invisible to current analytics.

## Pattern (cribbed from operator's production design)

For each queue boundary:
- Sender stamps a 'send_at_ns' (monotonic) at emit time
- Receiver stamps a 'receive_at_ns' at consume time
- Difference = queue-resident time
- A standalone timing event captures (boundary_id, send_at, receive_at) so analytics can aggregate per boundary

Operator's exact phrasing: 'the only place that latency can hide is in the buffers and network. They still are accounted for on round trips, but are only measurable by the send and recieve times on different services.'

## Implementation sketch

- Add a TimingEvent payload variant (or sidecar field) carrying boundary_id + send/receive monotonic timestamps
- Emit at every queue cross-point
- Analytics (mu-8ypx) aggregates: 'queue_latency_ms_p99 by boundary_id'

## Out of scope

- Cross-machine round-trip measurement (needs synchronized clocks — defer until mu has a real multi-daemon deployment story)
- Hardware timer (rdtscp) — std Instant is fine for v1; rdtscp is a follow-up if Instant overhead becomes measurable

## Cross-references

- mu-5g7i (TaskTelemetry — uses elapsed_ms today but no per-queue breakdown)
- mu-035 (ProviderStatusUpdate — already does this for one path; this bead generalizes)
- The 'event source identifier' bead (also from 2026-05-16 — same pattern conversation)

title: mu-tui: multi-line input expansion + buffer selection via xclip/editor escape
description:
## Problem

Two related input/output ergonomic gaps:

1. Input field is single-line; longer text scrolls sideways, making it
   hard to see/edit what's been typed. No surfaced way to use an external
   editor for longer messages.

2. No way to select parts of the transcript buffer for copy/paste into
   other programs. zellij solves this by dumping the whole buffer to the
   user's editor of choice via a screen+edit shortcut, plus xclip for
   clipboard integration.

## Proposal

### Input ergonomics

- Input field auto-expands from 1 line to up to 3 lines when text would
  otherwise scroll sideways
- Surface ctrl-x ctrl-e (or similar) for opening current input in $EDITOR
  for longer messages; tooltip/hint when input nears 3-line limit

### Buffer selection / clipboard

- Keyboard shortcut to dump current transcript buffer into $EDITOR
  (zellij-style) for selection/copy
- Integrate xclip (X11) / wl-copy (Wayland) / pbcopy (macOS) for direct
  buffer-to-clipboard flow when available
- Behavior respects runtime environment

## Acceptance

- Multi-line text input doesn't scroll sideways for typical message
  lengths (3 lines = ~240 chars at default width)
- ctrl-x ctrl-e (or chosen binding) opens current input in $EDITOR
- Buffer-to-editor shortcut works; can copy selected text into system
  clipboard via xclip on FreeBSD/Linux X11

## Cross-refs

- mu-to2 (mu-tui-input-history: arrow-up command history — different
  concern, same input field)
- Provenance: TUI feedback (mu daemon f3845422 session-2, 2026-05-15)

title: mu-7rk-b: Server-side BEARER auth handler + dispatcher (no enforcement)
description:
**mu-7rk decomposition phase B** — Server-side BEARER handler + dispatcher. **Records `AuthState` per connection but does NOT enforce it on protected RPCs (that's mu-7rk-c).**

## Why

With protocol types in mu-7rk-a, this bead lands the server-side handshake mechanics: a pluggable handler trait, BEARER implementation, and the dispatch wiring for `peer.auth_offer` / `peer.auth_initiate`. Enforcement and transport close are split into their own beads (mu-7rk-c, mu-7rk-d) so that this bead can land cleanly without the security/scope-expansion debate.

## Scope

### In

- `crates/mu-coding/src/serve/auth.rs` (NEW or update from v0 reference):
  - `AuthMechanismHandler` trait with `mechanism()`, `step_initial(initial_response: Option<&str>) -> AuthStepOutcome`, default `step_response()` rejecting multi-step
  - `AuthStepOutcome::{Done(Capability), Denied{code, reason}, Challenge{...}}`
  - `AuthRegistry` that **errors on duplicate mechanism registration** (per codex minor #1) — `AuthRegistry::new(handlers) -> Result<Self, DuplicateMechanismError>`
  - `BearerHandler`:
    - **Constant-time token comparison** via the `subtle` crate (per codex important #1) — store HMAC-SHA-256 digests of allowlisted tokens, compare candidate digests with `subtle::ConstantTimeEq`
    - **Token length cap** (e.g., 4096 bytes); reject with `MalformedExchange` (per codex important #2)
    - Default-empty allowlist fails closed
- `crates/mu-coding/src/serve/dispatch.rs`:
  - Handlers for `peer.auth_offer` and `peer.auth_initiate` RPC methods
  - Per-connection `AuthState` (records authentication; **NOT consumed by other handlers in this bead**)
- `mu-core::config::AuthConfig::Bearer { tokens: Vec<String> }`:
  - Custom `Debug` impl that **redacts tokens** (per codex important #5) — wrap in a `RedactedString` newtype or use `#[serde(skip)]` for Debug derivation
- Tests in `crates/mu-coding/tests/auth_smoke.rs`:
  - BEARER happy path (good token → `Done`)
  - BEARER bad-token rejection (bad token → `Denied{InvalidCredentials}`)
  - Unsupported-mechanism rejection (wire `Other("foo")` → `Denied{UnsupportedMechanism}`)
  - Malformed-exchange rejection (missing `initial_response` for BEARER)
  - Oversized-token rejection (per codex important #2)
  - Constant-time comparison (smoke test: timing variance bounded)
  - Duplicate-mechanism registration errors (per codex minor #1)

### Out

- **Enforcement of `AuthState` on session.*/mailbox.*/etc. RPCs** — that is mu-7rk-c (the load-bearing security bit)
- Transport close on Denied — mu-7rk-d
- Client-side flow — mu-7rk-e
- Rate limiting — mu-7rk-f
- Multi-step state registry (challenge/server_state_id machinery) — mu-7rk-g
- Any change to existing peer.hello / mailbox dispatch flow

## Acceptance

- All seven test cases above pass
- `cargo test --workspace` green; existing peer.hello + mailbox tests still green
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean
- `cargo fmt --check` clean
- New workspace dep: `subtle` (const-time comparison primitive); update root `Cargo.toml` `[workspace.dependencies]`. **One** new dep total. **No other** new workspace deps.
- Bearer token allowlist defaults to empty (fails closed for the BEARER handler itself)

## Universal don'ts

- Do NOT add a capability-enforcement check anywhere (that is mu-7rk-c)
- Do NOT add transport-close machinery (that is mu-7rk-d)
- Do NOT pin "unauthenticated session.* allowed" in tests — the v0 test that did this (per codex blocker #3) is the anti-pattern. Tests in this bead should be auth-mechanism-only and not assert anything about non-auth RPC behavior.

## References

- Codex review: `~/.claude-personal/experiments/codex-review-mu-7rk-output.txt`
  - Important #1 (constant-time HMAC), #2 (length cap), #5 (redact Debug), minor #1 (duplicate mechanism error)
- Reference diff (v0): jj rev `twztzykv 78978fdb` in `.delegations/mu-7rk-2026-05-15` — `BearerHandler` skeleton can be reused; replace `HashSet::contains` with HMAC + `subtle`
- Spec for SASL framing: RFC 4422 (conceptual model only — wire is JSON-RPC, not SASL)
- BEARER mechanism: RFC 7628
close_reason: Merged in PR #66 (real, this time) — mu-7rk-b BEARER handler + dispatcher. br dep graph still inverted (mu-5pn/mu-fnn/mu-oeo listed as 'blocking' when they're downstream consumers).

Scope: typed serde structures for the JSON catalog shape, materialized-vs-planned validation, and unit tests against a small fixture. This enables future aws-recon skill/broker work to resolve `AwsCapability.name` before materializing credentials.

title: mu CLI: 'mu tui' subcommand bails — every Opus reviewer concludes the TUI doesn't exist
description:
## Symptom (single most-impactful repo-perception issue per opus review)

In crates/mu-coding/src/bin/mu.rs:

  Command::Tui | Command::Orchestrate { .. } => {
      anyhow::bail!("this subcommand is not yet implemented; mu is pre-MVP. ...")
  }

But mu-tui exists as a fully-fleshed-out ~4500 LOC binary in crates/mu-tui/. Every Opus session that has read the repo (6+ so far per operator tally) concludes 'the TUI is aspirational, should be implemented or removed' — they don't know it's already the most-developed frontend.

## Why this matters

This is the primary cause of agent-perception drift: bail message + README 'TUI is intended surface' framing → reviewers reliably mis-describe the workspace shape. Every fresh agent burns turns re-discovering what exists.

## Three candidate fixes (pick one)

1. **Git-foo style exec**: 'mu tui' looks up 'mu-tui' on $PATH (or alongside its own binary) and execs it. Mirrors how git delegates to git-subcmd binaries. Lowest friction.
2. **Feature-gate into mu-coding**: pull mu-tui under mu-coding as a feature, expose Command::Tui as a real subcommand. Heavier; tightens the dep graph.
3. **Update the bail message** at minimum: 'Run mu-tui --mu-binary $(which mu) — the TUI is a separate binary in this workspace.' Buys 30 seconds; doesn't fix the underlying perception gap.

Author preference (per opus review): #1 — git-foo lookup.

## Acceptance

- 'mu tui' starts the TUI (or prints a one-liner pointing at mu-tui)
- Fresh-read agents stop concluding the TUI is unimplemented
- README's status section can drop the 'TUI is aspirational' framing

## Source

Surfaced 2026-05-17 by opus-4-7 review (session 3262c036eaca7daa, event 194). Quoted: 'The current state is the worst of all worlds: the code exists, but newcomers (human or agent) reliably mis-describe it.'

title: mu: discovery + permission model for memory/MCP/skills (information-leak-resistant)
description:
## Problem

mu has (or will have) several extensible surfaces: memory entries, MCP
tools (mu-yc6 / mu-71q), skills, and capability-gated resources. Agents
need a way to list/discover what's available to them, but the discovery
mechanism itself is a security boundary:

- Naively exposing the full list (with blocked items marked) leaks the
  existence of restricted capabilities, which is itself information
  disclosure
- A multi-tenant or supervisor-of-many-agents setup amplifies this: an
  agent learning "there's an MCP server called `aws-prod-admin` but you
  can't use it" gives them attack surface they wouldn't otherwise have

## Design

### Two policies, default is the safer one

(a) **Filtered discovery (default):** agents see only what they have
    access to. The existence of items outside their capability set is
    not disclosed.

(b) **Marked discovery (opt-in):** agents see the full list with blocked
    items marked as inaccessible. Useful in single-tenant trusted
    contexts where the operator wants visibility into the full surface.
    Not the default. Marked as a security trade-off in docs.

Configuration is per-capability-axis (memory, MCP, skills) and per-agent-
context, so an enterprise can choose (a) for production multi-agent
topologies and (b) for development.

### Surfaces this applies to

- **Memory entries**: `recall` / list operations filter by capability
- **MCP tools**: tool-list / introspection filters by capability (ties
  into mu-yc6's tool registration)
- **Skills**: skill discovery filters by capability
- **Generally, anything capability-gated**: discovery surface enforces
  the same gate as the use surface

### Information-leak resistance

- Don't return counts that disclose existence ("3 available, 7 filtered"
  leaks)
- Don't return placeholder slots ("[restricted]" entries leak)
- Don't return error messages that confirm existence ("permission denied
  for X" leaks; "no such X" is the response for both nonexistent and
  inaccessible)

### Discovery capability separable from use capability

Discovery is itself a capability ("can-list-memories", "can-list-mcp-
tools"), separable from the use capability. An agent might be able to
USE a specific item without being able to LIST the broader set, or vice
versa, depending on operator policy.

## Acceptance

- `recall list`, `tools list`, `skills list` (or equivalent surfaces)
  filter by capability under policy (a) by default
- Policy (b) opt-in is configurable per-axis and per-agent-context
- Filtered discovery does not disclose existence of inaccessible items
  via counts, placeholders, or error message differentiation
- Discovery capability and use capability are separable

## Cross-refs

- mu-hh1 (SupervisorCapability axis — substrate for the capability checks)
- mu-7rk-* (capability decomposition — provides gating primitives)
- mu-yc6 (MCP client — needs to enforce discovery filtering on tool list)
- mu-71q (MCP server adapter — needs to enforce on outbound tool exposure)
- memory 03b44633 (capability broker pattern)
- Companion to mu-memory bead (filed same session)
- Provenance: user feedback session 2026-05-15

title: BUG: cargo test --workspace fails intermittently — env-var mutation in tool tests races with parallel test execution
description:
## Symptom

`cargo test --workspace` fails non-deterministically; observed today (2026-05-11 ~19:00 local) with:

- tools::grep::tests::b4_count_mode
- tools::grep::tests::b6_case_insensitive

Same assertion: `assert!(!result.is_error)`. `cargo test -p mu-coding tools::grep` alone passes (11/11). Running b4 in isolation also passes. Task 19603420 reported all 278 workspace tests passing at 14:59 — the race window has just widened recently (more total tests = higher concurrency pressure).

## Root cause

`crates/mu-coding/src/tools/grep.rs::tests::rg_unavailable_returns_clean_error` (line 534) mutates the process-global `MU_RG_BINARY` env var:

```rust
std::env::set_var("MU_RG_BINARY", "/no/such/binary/rg");
// ... test runs ...
std::env::set_var("MU_RG_BINARY", original);  // or remove_var
```

Parallel `#[tokio::test]`s in the same test binary call `locate_rg()` which reads `MU_RG_BINARY`. Any test reading the var during the bogus-value window fails. b4 / b6 lose this race more often than b5; bash tests don't currently fail because their env keys (`MU_TEST_BASH_*`) don't interact with the rg path, but the same anti-pattern exists.

## Same anti-pattern elsewhere

`crates/mu-coding/src/tools/bash.rs` has two tests that mutate process env:

- tests::b5_strict_env_scrub (line 541): sets `MU_TEST_BASH_API_KEY`
- tests::b10_yolo_env_passes_through (line 598): sets `MU_TEST_BASH_YOLO_VAR`

These currently don't race against anything (no concurrent reader of those keys) but the pattern is fragile.

## Rust 1.74+ context

`std::env::set_var` is documented as not thread-safe and is `unsafe` from Rust 2024 edition onward. `cargo test` runs tests in threads by default. So any `set_var` in a test is a future-trap.

## Fix options

A. **Minimal (chosen for triage)**: `#[ignore]` the actual offender; document follow-up here. Loses one test path's coverage; that path ("rg not found") is structurally trivial. Done in commit landing alongside this bead.

B. **Durable**: refactor `locate_rg` so the rg binary path can be injected at `GrepTool` construction time. Tests pass a bogus path directly, no env mutation needed. Same shape applies to bash.rs (inject env scrub policy / passthrough policy as struct config rather than process env). Estimate: ~1 hour each.

C. **Standard fix**: add `serial_test` to workspace dev-deps; `#[serial]` the three offenders. Quick, adds one dep. Doesn't fix the root cause (env mutation in tests) — just serializes it.

## Suggested

Land A now (in this conversation's commit). Open follow-up beads referencing this one to do B for grep and B for bash separately.
close_reason: done

title: mu-ai (Anthropic): per-message output_tokens undercounted ~100x — final stream usage not captured
description:
## Symptom

Operator ran a 10-turn opus-4-7 'project review' in mu-tui (2026-05-17, daemon 3262c036eaca7daa). F5 metrics showed:

  out tok: 257
  in tok: 1,456,208

across 37 tool calls + 25 assistant messages. 257 output is ~100x too low: event 194 alone has 21,781 chars of assistant text (~5,500 tokens) but its message.usage.output_tokens reports 1.

## Per-message breakdown (proof)

From ~/.local/share/mu/events/3262c036eaca7daa/session-1.jsonl:

  event 194 text_len=21781  output_tokens=1
  event 228 text_len=8690   output_tokens=1
  event 221 text_len=6585   output_tokens=2
  event 214 text_len=4659   output_tokens=5
  event 235 text_len=4506   output_tokens=3
  event 249 text_len=3226   output_tokens=1
  event 242 text_len=2291   output_tokens=6
  event 158 text_len=820    output_tokens=1

text_len = chars. With ~4 chars/token, event 194 should be ~5,500 output_tokens, not 1. The ratio is consistent across all long messages: we're grabbing 1-6 tokens regardless of actual length.

## Hypothesis

Anthropic's streaming API emits multiple message_delta events with cumulative usage.output_tokens, terminated by a message_stop. We are likely:

(a) reading output_tokens from the FIRST message_delta (which contains only the initial 1-2 tokens), not the LAST, OR
(b) reading from a non-cumulative delta field, OR
(c) reading from message_start.usage (which only sets the input baseline + cache breakdown; output starts at ~0).

Code to inspect: crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs (or wherever the stream-delta loop builds the final Usage struct).

## Why now

This has been present since the static-demo era. F5 budget is also hardcoded (/usr/local/bin/zsh.00 / .00 per main.rs:376) — cost calculator not wired up. The output-token undercount has been masked by the absent cost calc. Operator noticed today during a deep-review session.

## Acceptance

- Final message.usage.output_tokens reflects the full output for streamed messages on Anthropic
- Same per-Done usage gets aggregated in event_log::cumulative_usage and surfaces in daemon.stats.total_output_tokens
- Test: a streamed Anthropic response of N tokens reports output_tokens >= N (within 5%)
- F5 metrics show reasonable output counts on real-world sessions

## Cross-refs

- main.rs:1448 — cost_usd: 0.0 hardcoded (related but separate; pricing-table wiring)
- event_log::cumulative_usage (correct logic, garbage in → garbage out)
- handlers/daemon.rs:67-68 — saturating_add aggregation (correct)
- Discovered in session-1.jsonl of daemon 3262c036eaca7daa (preserved for forensics)
close_reason: Merged in PR #75 (2650fd1d → main as commit per gh, auto-merge --rebase). All 4 CI checks green (fmt + clippy + test + verify-claims) at 2026-05-17T19:41:04Z. Regression test mu_yz48_message_delta_top_level_usage_is_captured passes.

title: mu-delegate-mailbox-back-channel: pre-issue mailbox.post handle from child→parent at session.delegate time
description:
Extend `session.delegate` (mu-031) so child sessions receive a pre-issued `mailbox.post` peer handle to their parent at creation time. Eliminates the awkward `peer.hello` round-trip a child has to do just to talk back to the parent that spawned it.

## Use case

An orchestrator O calls `session.delegate { parent_session_id: O, provider: ..., ... }` to spawn a child worker W. W is going to post results / status updates / questions back to O via `mailbox.post`. Today W has to:

1. Wait until it's running.
2. Discover O's session_id (it's its own `parent_session_id` from the delegate event).
3. Call `peer.hello { to_session_id: O, want: { method: "mailbox.post" } }` against O.
4. Receive a handle.
5. THEN it can post.

That's a 3-step bootstrap every child does, against the parent it was just born from. Parent-child relationships are already known at delegate time — the handle could be issued THEN.

## Scope

### In

- Extend `DelegateSessionRequest` with `grant_back_channel: bool` (default `true` for explicit-orchestrator workflows; `false` for the legacy "child doesn't talk to parent" case).
- When `grant_back_channel = true`:
  - At `session.delegate` dispatch time, the parent session's `MailboxState::issue_handle` is called with `peer_session_id = <child's session_id>` and `allowed_methods = {"mailbox.post"}` (and optionally a budget / expiry — see "Open" below).
  - The handle token is returned in a new `DelegateSessionResponse.back_channel_handle: Option<String>` field.
- The child session needs the token to be visible to it. Options (decide as part of the bead):
  - (a) Returned only in the dispatch response — the orchestrator forwards it to the child via the child's input channel as part of session setup.
  - (b) Recorded in the child's event log at session-creation time so the child can find it via `session.events`.
  - (c) Exposed via a new per-session RPC like `session.back_channel` returning the child's pre-issued handle.

  Lean: (b) — event-log-recorded means it survives restart (composes with mu-mh4) and is discoverable without an extra RPC.

### Out

- Multi-parent back-channels (a child has at most one delegating parent; that's the only back-channel granted automatically).
- Granting back-channels via `peer.hello` after the fact (the parent can already do this manually).
- Auto-renewal of expired back-channel handles.

## Open decisions (capture in PR)

- **Should the back-channel handle have an expiry by default?** Lean: no — match the child's session lifetime. Parent can revoke explicitly later (when revocation lands; out of scope here).
- **Should the back-channel handle have a call budget by default?** Lean: no — orchestrator workflows want unbounded child→parent posts (status updates, progress). Operators who want bounding can pass `back_channel_max_calls: Option<u32>` in the delegate request.
- **What if the child later calls `peer.hello` against the parent itself?** Should be allowed; multiple handles per (caller, target) is fine. No de-duplication needed.

## Acceptance

- `DelegateSessionResponse.back_channel_handle` is `Some(token)` when `grant_back_channel = true` (default).
- The child session can locate its back-channel handle via the chosen discovery mechanism (likely event log).
- The child can call `mailbox.post { to_session_id: parent, peer_handle: back_channel, ... }` immediately after spawn, with no intervening `peer.hello`.
- Test: delegate-then-immediate-post round-trip works without any `peer.hello` from the child.
- Existing mu-031 tests still pass; the new field is opt-in default-true but legacy callers can pass `false` for byte-exact backwards compatibility.

## Composition

- Builds on mu-031 (closed) and mu-lho (closed).
- Composes with "result-correlation fields" follow-up: the back-channel handle is what a child uses to post results; correlation fields document what shape those results take.
- Composes with the future "supervisor capability axis": grant_back_channel is one slice of the broader "supervised session relationship."

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-14 / 2026-05-15. Surfaced during mu-lho orchestrator exploration as the second of three Tier-1 follow-ups.

title: mu-tui-bubbletea-reference: evaluate borrowing MVU / command / widget patterns from bubbletea-rs ecosystem
description:
Pattern-reference bead: evaluate whether mu's TUI could benefit from MVU / command / widget patterns from the bubbletea-rs Rust ecosystem. Not an adoption bead — the framework itself is pre-1.0 (v0.0.9 as of 2026-05-13) and mu's TUI is already substantial (~2800 LOC in main.rs alone, multiple open TUI beads).

## Reference

Repo: https://github.com/whit3rabbit/bubbletea-rs

Ecosystem:
- bubbletea-rs (v0.0.9) — core MVU framework, async runtime
- bubbletea-widgets (v0.1.12) — 13+ pre-built widgets (spinners, inputs, tables, progress, etc.)
- lipgloss-extras (v0.1.1) — styling DSL (colors, gradients, layouts)

Inspired by Charm's Bubble Tea (Go), which itself draws on Elm Architecture.

## What might be worth borrowing (NOT the framework wholesale)

1. **MVU pattern.** Pure-functional state transitions + commands that return future messages. If mu's TUI does not already follow MVU naturally, this is a cleaner refactor target than ad-hoc event loops.
2. **Command abstraction.** Async operations returning future messages instead of spawning tasks and threading channels. Could simplify how mu handles long-running async work in the TUI.
3. **Widget composition idioms.** How bubbletea-widgets composes widgets into a view tree could inform panel composition for mu-pex / mu-ib5 / mu-cha / mu-jb3.

## What argues against adoption

- bubbletea-rs is v0.0.9 — pre-stable, APIs explicitly stated to be "still evolving"
- mu's TUI is established and being actively developed (multiple open TUI beads)
- ratatui is the de facto Rust TUI; switching to a wrapper invites maintenance churn
- lipgloss-extras's styling is duplicative with ratatui's built-in Style/Modifier/Color

## Trigger to actually pick this up

When mu's TUI hits friction that MVU would clearly solve, or when starting a new substantial TUI surface where greenfield architecture choice is open. Until then: reference only.

## Notes

Filed 2026-05-13 after Thaddeus shared the link as 'don't know if it'd be useful to use some of the constructs.' Not urgent; placeholder so the reference doesn't get lost.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Additional TUI pattern reference (filed 2026-05-13 alongside bubbletea-rs): **oatmeal** — https://github.com/dustinblackman/oatmeal. Rust + ratatui terminal UI chat app for LLMs.

Specifically worth mining when working on:
- mu-pex / mu-jb3 / mu-ib5: panel/render TUI work — oatmeal's chat-bubble rendering is concrete reference for conversation display.
- Multi-backend provider abstraction: oatmeal's backend layer (ChatGPT / Ollama) is a smaller-scope analog of mu's provider abstraction.
- Slash command UX: oatmeal has slash commands; mu has Ctrl-X Ctrl-P (mu-62s); compare ergonomics.
- Neovim integration: oatmeal has a real Neovim plugin path. mu-tui currently has $EDITOR-handoff (Ctrl-X Ctrl-P) but no deeper editor integration. Could be a future bead direction.
- Themes: oatmeal supports themes. mu has color polish in mu-u8a (TUI color polish — moonfly/github_dark family).

This is reference material, not adoption material — like bubbletea-rs, oatmeal is its own application with its own architectural choices. Worth pattern-mining for specific surfaces, not wholesale framework borrowing.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Concrete widget candidate (filed 2026-05-13): **throbber-widgets-tui** — https://github.com/arkbig/throbber-widgets-tui. Direct fit for rendering mu-035's ProviderStatusSnapshot states as animated spinner+label. Filed as its own implementable bead (separate from this reference shelf) because it's a small concrete adopt-the-crate task, not pattern-mining. See mu-tui-throbber bead.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Three more ratatui showcase widgets surveyed 2026-05-13 (rapid-fire by operator browsing https://ratatui.rs/showcase/third-party-widgets/):

**1. tui-scrollview** — https://github.com/joshka/tui-widgets/tree/main/tui-scrollview
Simple scrollable views. Target beads where this directly fits:
- mu-ib5 (F9 mailbox view) — long mailbox lists almost certainly need scrolling
- Conversation history rendering generally (whatever mu-tui does today for long histories)

**2. tui-term** — https://github.com/a-kenji/tui-term
Embedded terminal emulator as a ratatui widget. Bigger architectural fit:
- Render mu's bash tool output as a LIVE terminal pane (vs. capturing stdout and rendering as text). Would let the user see commands execute in real-time, with proper terminal control sequence rendering (color, cursor, etc.).
- Could also replace the $EDITOR-handoff (Ctrl-X Ctrl-P, mu-62s) by embedding the editor in a pane instead of suspending the whole TUI.
- Bigger lift than a typical widget; worth its own design bead before commit.

**3. tui-tree-widget** — https://github.com/EdJoPaTo/tui-rs-tree-widget (or successor)
Tree view widget. **Direct fit for mu-cha (TUI: implement F2 session tree view)** — that bead is literally about rendering the session tree.

Pattern across all four widgets surfaced today (throbber + these three): ratatui's third-party widget ecosystem is rich enough that several open TUI beads have ready-made off-the-shelf primitives. Worth a sweep through the ratatui showcase before greenfield-building each bead.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
**Charmbracelet** (https://github.com/charmbracelet) — design-language reference (added 2026-05-13). All projects are Go; Rust ports exist but are third-party. Their projects ranked by mu-relevance:

- **Bubble Tea / Bubbles / Lip Gloss** (Go) — MVU framework / widgets / styling. Already covered: bubbletea-rs port noted earlier in this bead.
- **Glamour** (Go) — Markdown-in-terminal renderer. **Concrete fit for mu**: filed as separate bead (mu-tui-markdown) using termimad as the Rust analog.
- **VHS** (Go) — terminal recorder for making demo GIFs. Not on mu's critical path; relevant only if/when mu has a public README with demo content.
- **Mods** (Go) — AI on the command line. Adjacent to mu / claude-code / oatmeal but server-driven and Go-stack. Reference for product-shape ideas, not architecture.
- **Gum** (Go) — shell-friendly TUI commands. Not relevant to mu (mu is its own TUI, not a shell-script helper).

The brand pattern: Charmbracelet cultivates terminal aesthetic as a design language (consistent typography in banners, VHS-recorded GIFs, careful color palettes). Useful as design-philosophy reference even when not adopting specific libs.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
**Crush** (https://github.com/charmbracelet/crush) — most directly mu-shaped product Charmbracelet ships. Multi-model TUI coding agent (Go, built on their Bubble Tea / Lip Gloss / Glamour stack).

## Feature mapping to mu (current state + open beads)

| Crush | mu state |
|---|---|
| Multi-model (OpenAI/Anthropic-compatible + custom) | ✓ mu has anthropic / openai-codex / openrouter providers |
| Switch LLMs mid-session preserving context | mu-4ge (open, P3) — session.set_provider RPC. **Direct match.** |
| Session-based, multiple per project | ✓ mu has (mu-935 FileBackend, mu-cha session tree view) |
| LSP-enhanced context | mu-f6z **needs re-scoping** — crush uses LSP for *automatic context enrichment*, not just agent-queryable lookup. Bigger primitive than I originally captured. |
| MCP-extensible (http/stdio/sse) | **No mu equivalent.** Filed as new bead. |
| Cross-platform incl. BSDs | mu works on FreeBSD; BSD coverage otherwise untested |

## UX patterns worth studying (when looking at the demo)

- How mid-session model switch is surfaced in the UI (mu-4ge will need this decision)
- How LSP enhancement is exposed — silently inject context, or surface a 'context: file.rs:42 included' callout?
- How MCP tool registrations are listed / managed in-session
- Session-tree presentation (compare with mu-cha's planned F2 view)

## Worth noting

- Crush is Go; mu is Rust. Direct adoption isn't possible. Reference value is UX/architecture.
- They market 'Industrial Grade' / '25k+ applications' — claims to validate. Crush itself is newer.
- Their LSP-context-enrichment approach is the strongest take I've seen on 'agents should see semantic code'. Mu-f6z benefits from this framing.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
**Harness-audit catalog 2026-05-14** (field-awareness, NOT roadmap items):

Per OpenAI / Anthropic / LangChain / harness-engineering.ai articles, these are widely-cited harness primitives mu doesn't have. Filed here as awareness only — if symptoms surface later, the prior art is named:

- **Dynamic tool scoping per phase / skill** (Vercel: removing 80% of tools measurably improved performance). mu exposes all tools to every session; skills could gate. SkillActivation SpanKind exists; runtime loader doesn't.
- **Compaction runtime** for the rope. Compaction SpanKind landed in mu-ovl; auto-emission when context fills + demoting old spans isn't wired.
- **Tool output offloading**: head/tail kept in context, full output to filesystem. LangChain pattern. Useful for long-bash-output scenarios.

What we explicitly DIDN'T file as feature beads (after antagonistic-check pass):

- Checkpoint-resume with precondition validation — too abstract for now; events are the substrate; precondition revalidation is implementation-detail-ish
- Background gardening (recurring /goal cleanup runs) — useful but not yet needed at our scale
- AGENTS.md auto-injection — already covered in spirit by per-repo goal-protocol.md

The ONE thing we DID file as actionable: per-step verification discipline (P2) — see mu-(whatever the new ID is) — because the compounding-failure math (95%^20 = 36%) is concretely backed by our /goal experiment failures, not just article claims.

Doc/spec only unless a tiny bead reference is needed.
